meta.description: AI Assist audio scripts for NOA Dashboard hover explanations

elements.label: Patient Cohort Stat Card
elements.script: This study analyzed 2,413 patients from the Royan Institute, one of the largest fertility centers in the Middle East. All patients had confirmed non-obstructive azoospermia and underwent micro-TESE — a surgical procedure to retrieve sperm directly from testicular tissue. The cohort size of over 2,400 patients is notably large for this field. Most published studies on sperm retrieval prediction use fewer than 500 patients, so this dataset provides significantly more statistical power and generalizability.

elements.label: Feature Set Stat Card
elements.script: The feature set started with 55 original clinical variables from the Royan Institute database. Through careful feature engineering — particularly the extraction of 18 bilateral pathology features from free-text pathology reports — we expanded this to 73 total features. These 18 engineered pathology features turned out to be among the most important predictors in the final model, which highlights why domain-specific feature engineering is so critical in medical machine learning.

elements.label: Pathology Features Stat Card
elements.script: This card highlights the eighteen pathology features engineered from free-text biopsy reports. These include eight categories — SCO, CSTH, four stages of maturation arrest, hypospermatogenesis, and severe hypospermatogenesis — extracted separately for both the right and left testis. This bilateral extraction approach is novel in sperm retrieval prediction research. Most prior studies use only a single overall pathology classification, but separating right and left tissue patterns allows the model to detect laterality-specific signals that would otherwise be lost.

elements.label: Success Rate Stat Card
elements.script: The overall sperm retrieval success rate in this cohort was 36.7 percent — that's 886 successful cases out of 2,413 total. This means roughly one in three patients had successful sperm retrieval. This class imbalance — with about twice as many failures as successes — is important for model training and is why we used stratified sampling and appropriate evaluation metrics like AUC rather than simple accuracy.

elements.label: Outcome Distribution Pie Chart
elements.script: This pie chart shows the binary outcome distribution for our 2,413-patient cohort. The green segment represents 886 patients with successful sperm retrieval, while the red segment shows 1,527 patients where no sperm was found during micro-TESE. This roughly 37 to 63 percent split creates a moderate class imbalance. To handle this, our modeling pipeline used stratified cross-validation to ensure both classes were proportionally represented in every training and test fold.

elements.label: Pathology SHAP Importance Bar Chart
elements.script: This bar chart displays the SHAP importance values specifically for the pathology features in our CatBoost v2 model. SHAP — which stands for SHapley Additive exPlanations — measures how much each feature contributes to individual predictions. Notice that right testis severe hypospermatogenesis has the highest SHAP value at 0.396, followed by right testis hypospermatogenesis and left testis severe hypospermatogenesis. This confirms that testicular tissue histology — particularly the severity of spermatogenic failure — is a crucial predictor of sperm retrieval success. The dominance of pathology features here validates our decision to extract bilateral pathology information from the original free-text reports.

elements.label: Per-Variable Missingness Bar Chart
elements.script: This horizontal bar chart shows the percentage of missing values for each variable retained after clinical curation. In real-world clinical datasets, missingness is inevitable — some lab tests aren't always ordered, some fields aren't always recorded. Variables with high missingness — like previous TESE outcome at over 21 percent — were handled through KNN imputation, which estimates missing values based on similar patients. Importantly, imputation was fitted only on training folds to prevent data leakage — a critical methodological safeguard.

elements.label: Cleaning Steps List
elements.script: These are the sequential data cleaning steps applied to the raw clinical dataset. Starting from ingesting the original Excel workbook, we went through column inventory, missingness quantification, duplicate removal, and data type standardization. Each step was carefully documented and is reproducible. A key decision was removing the IQR Clipper from the v2 pipeline — in the original v1 pipeline, the IQR Clipper inadvertently zeroed out pathology features, causing data leakage that inflated model performance artificially.

elements.label: 18 Pathology Features Grid
elements.script: This grid lists all 18 bilateral pathology features engineered from the original free-text pathology reports. They are split into right testis features — prefixed with RT — and left testis features — prefixed with LT. Categories include Sertoli Cell-Only syndrome, hypospermatogenesis at different severity levels, various stages of maturation arrest — from spermatogonial to elongated — and CSTH, which stands for Complete Spermatogenic Tubular Hyalinization. This bilateral extraction was a novel contribution of this research, as previous studies typically used only a single pathology label.

elements.label: Transformations Table
elements.script: This table documents every data transformation applied during preprocessing. Key transformations include RobustScaler for feature scaling — chosen over StandardScaler because it's less sensitive to outliers — ordinal encoding with the detailed-plus-raw strategy for pathology variables, and KNN imputation for missing values. The v2 pipeline specifically removed the IQR Clipper that was present in v1, as it was identified as the source of data leakage during the virtual defense review.

elements.label: Publication-Quality Pipeline Flowchart
elements.script: This is the publication-quality flowchart showing the complete 16-step data cleaning and preprocessing pipeline. It's designed to be included in journal submissions and provides a visual overview of every decision made from raw data to model-ready features. The flowchart clearly shows the data flow from the original 2,413-patient Royan Institute dataset through cleaning, feature engineering, pathology extraction, and scaling — culminating in the CatBoost v2 model achieving an AUC of 0.8306. You can download this in PDF, SVG, or PNG format for your publications.

elements.label: Primary vs Salvage microTESE
elements.script: This section compares outcomes between primary and salvage micro-TESE procedures. Primary micro-TESE refers to the first surgical attempt at sperm retrieval, while salvage procedures are repeat attempts after a previous failed micro-TESE. Comparing these subgroups is clinically important because salvage cases typically have lower success rates due to prior surgical trauma and tissue scarring. Understanding these differences helps clinicians counsel patients more accurately about their individual chances of success.

elements.label: Models Tested Stat Card
elements.script: We benchmarked a total of 16 different machine learning algorithms for predicting sperm retrieval success. This comprehensive evaluation included tree-based methods like Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost; ensemble approaches like Stacking and Voting; neural networks including MLP and TabNet; linear models like Logistic Regression, Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net; and other approaches including SVM, KNN, Decision Tree, and Naive Bayes. Testing this many diverse algorithms ensures we find the best-performing approach rather than just assuming one model type will work best.

elements.label: Finalized Models Stat Card
elements.script: From the initial 16 models tested, 5 were retained in the final v2 pathology-integrated pipeline: CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, and Logistic Regression. These five were selected based on their performance across multiple metrics — AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and calibration. The final comparison among these five models clearly showed CatBoost as the top performer.

elements.label: Best Model Stat Card
elements.script: CatBoost emerged as the best-performing model in the v2 pipeline. CatBoost — which stands for Categorical Boosting — is a gradient boosting algorithm developed by Yandex. It excels at handling categorical features natively and has built-in mechanisms to prevent overfitting, such as ordered boosting. In our study, CatBoost's ability to naturally handle the mix of continuous clinical measurements and categorical pathology variables made it particularly well-suited for this dataset.

elements.label: Best AUC Stat Card
elements.script: The best AUC achieved was 0.8306 with a 95 percent confidence interval from 0.823 to 0.845. AUC — or Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve — measures a model's ability to distinguish between successful and unsuccessful sperm retrieval. An AUC of 0.83 means that if you randomly pick one patient who had successful retrieval and one who didn't, the model would correctly rank them 83 percent of the time. For a clinical prediction model in reproductive medicine, this represents strong discriminative performance.

elements.label: Model Roster Grid
elements.script: This grid shows all 16 machine learning algorithms that were evaluated in our benchmark. Each model was trained using nested cross-validation with hyperparameter tuning via randomized search. The models span diverse algorithmic families — from simple interpretable models like Logistic Regression and Decision Tree, to complex ensemble methods like Stacking and Voting, to modern gradient boosting frameworks like CatBoost, XGBoost, and LightGBM. This diversity ensures we capture different approaches to modeling the complex relationship between clinical features and sperm retrieval outcomes.

elements.label: Top Model Comparison Table
elements.script: This table provides a detailed side-by-side comparison of the top-performing models. For each model, you can see AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, and Brier score. CatBoost leads with an AUC of 0.8306, followed closely by XGBoost at 0.8214 and LightGBM at 0.8213. Notice that while the AUC values are similar among the top three, CatBoost also achieves the best balance between sensitivity — correctly identifying successful cases — and specificity — correctly identifying unsuccessful cases. The Brier score measures calibration quality — how well predicted probabilities match actual outcomes — with lower values being better.

elements.label: Validation Design Stat Card
elements.script: We used nested cross-validation as our primary validation strategy — this is a rigorous two-loop design where the outer loop evaluates model performance and the inner loop tunes hyperparameters. This prevents the common pitfall of overfitting during hyperparameter selection, which would inflate performance estimates. Nested CV is considered the gold standard for model evaluation when external validation data isn't available, and it's especially important in medical AI where overly optimistic performance claims can have real clinical consequences.

elements.label: Bootstrap Stat Card
elements.script: One thousand bootstrap iterations were performed on the top models to estimate confidence intervals for their performance metrics. Bootstrap resampling works by repeatedly sampling with replacement from the test data and computing the metric each time. This gives us a distribution of possible values rather than a single point estimate, resulting in the 95 percent confidence intervals you see reported — for example, CatBoost's AUC of 0.8306 with a CI of 0.823 to 0.845. This tells us we can be quite confident the true AUC lies within that narrow range.

elements.label: Clinical Utility (DCA) Stat Card
elements.script: Decision Curve Analysis — or DCA — evaluates the clinical utility of our prediction model by comparing it against two default strategies: treating all patients and treating no patients. DCA plots net benefit across different threshold probabilities, showing at which risk levels our model provides better clinical decisions than either default strategy. Our top models showed net benefit exceeding both baselines across a clinically meaningful threshold range of roughly 5 to 60 percent, meaning the model adds genuine value for clinical decision-making in preoperative counseling.

elements.label: Best Calibration Stat Card
elements.script: The Brier score measures how well a model's predicted probabilities match actual outcomes — it's essentially the mean squared error between predicted probabilities and the binary outcome. A Brier score of zero means perfect calibration, while 0.25 represents no skill. CatBoost achieved a Brier score of approximately 0.20, indicating reasonably good calibration. This is important because in clinical settings, a well-calibrated model means that when it predicts a 70 percent chance of success, roughly 70 percent of such patients actually do have successful sperm retrieval.

elements.label: Brier Score Model Grid
elements.script: This grid compares the Brier scores across all validated models. Remember, lower Brier scores indicate better calibration — meaning the predicted probabilities are more trustworthy. CatBoost and the ensemble models generally achieve the best calibration. Good calibration is especially critical for the CDSS tool on this dashboard, where clinicians use predicted probabilities to counsel patients about their chances of successful sperm retrieval.

elements.label: Decision Curve Analysis Text
elements.script: This section provides the detailed interpretation of the Decision Curve Analysis results. The key finding is that our model's net benefit consistently exceeds the treat-all and treat-none strategies across a wide range of clinically relevant threshold probabilities. In practical terms, this means using our model to guide micro-TESE decisions would lead to better outcomes than either performing the surgery on everyone or on no one — validating its use as a clinical decision support tool.

elements.label: XAI Method Stat Card
elements.script: SHAP — SHapley Additive exPlanations — is our primary explainability method. It's based on game theory, specifically Shapley values, which fairly distribute the 'credit' for a prediction among all input features. Unlike simpler methods like feature importance, SHAP provides both global explanations — which features matter overall — and local explanations — why the model made a specific prediction for a specific patient. We used TreeExplainer, which is optimized for tree-based models like CatBoost and computes exact Shapley values efficiently.

elements.label: XAI Best Model Stat Card
elements.script: All explainability analysis in the v2 pipeline is based on CatBoost, our best-performing model with AUC 0.8306. It's important to note that the v1 pipeline used LightGBM, but those results were invalidated due to data leakage from the IQR Clipper bug that zeroed pathology features. The v2 CatBoost SHAP analysis reveals a fundamentally different feature ranking — with pathology features now properly contributing — providing a clinically valid picture of what drives sperm retrieval predictions.

elements.label: Pathology Features Stat Card
elements.script: Eighteen bilateral pathology features were extracted from the original free-text pathology reports. These features — covering both right testis and left testis patterns — include SCO, hypospermatogenesis, various maturation arrest stages, and CSTH. In the v2 pipeline, these pathology features rank among the top predictors, which makes clinical sense because testicular histology directly reflects the state of spermatogenesis and is the strongest biological indicator of whether viable sperm can be found.

elements.label: Top-5 SHAP Features Grid
elements.script: These are the top 5 features by global SHAP importance in the CatBoost v2 model. LH — luteinizing hormone — ranks first, which is clinically significant because LH directly stimulates testosterone production in the testes and reflects the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis function. Age is second, reflecting the well-known decline in spermatogenic capacity with age. FSH — follicle stimulating hormone — is third, another key hormonal marker. Testosterone levels rank fourth. These top features align well with clinical knowledge about factors influencing spermatogenesis.

elements.label: Global SHAP Top-10 List
elements.script: This list shows the top 10 features ranked by mean absolute SHAP value across all predictions. The SHAP values represent the average magnitude of each feature's contribution to model output. Notice how hormonal markers — LH, FSH, Testosterone — and demographic features — Age, Body Weight — dominate the top ranks. Importantly, Partner Age is not in this list — it was excluded from the v2 pipeline as it had no clinical plausibility for predicting the male patient's sperm retrieval outcome. Its high ranking in v1 was an artifact of the data leakage.

elements.label: CatBoost Feature Importance Top-10
elements.script: This shows CatBoost's native feature importance — calculated during model training based on how often and how effectively each feature is used in tree splits. While SHAP values give a more nuanced picture, native feature importance provides a complementary view. Comparing SHAP and native importance helps validate that the important features are consistently ranked across different measurement approaches, increasing our confidence in the feature rankings.

elements.label: V2 Model Comparison Table
elements.script: This table presents the final v2 model comparison for the 5 retained models. CatBoost leads with AUC 0.8306, followed by XGBoost at 0.8214 and LightGBM at 0.8213. Note the relatively tight clustering — the top three are separated by less than one percentage point in AUC. However, CatBoost's advantage extends beyond AUC: it also shows the best specificity at 0.823, meaning it's the most accurate at identifying patients who will not have successful sperm retrieval. This is clinically important because it reduces unnecessary surgical procedures.

elements.label: Best Model Summary Text
elements.script: This summary confirms CatBoost as the best model from the v2 pathology-integrated pipeline. The selection path — 16 models tested, then narrowed to 5 for final comparison — ensures thoroughness. The pathology signal is highlighted as a significant contributor, with 18 features extracted from free-text reports. This pathology-aware approach is one of the novel contributions of this research, as most existing studies in NOA prediction rely only on hormonal and demographic variables.

elements.label: Pathology Feature Groups List
elements.script: This section categorizes the pathology features used in the v2 pipeline into logical groups. Severity scores capture an overall measure of spermatogenic impairment for each testis. SCO percentages indicate the proportion of seminiferous tubules showing Sertoli Cell-Only pattern — where germ cells are absent. Hypospermatogenesis measures reduced but not absent sperm production. Maturation arrest captures cases where sperm development stops at various stages. And CSTH — Complete Spermatogenic Tubular Hyalinization — represents the most severe form of testicular damage. Each of these pathology patterns has distinct clinical implications for sperm retrieval success.

elements.label: CDSS Introduction Video
elements.script: This introduction video explains how the Clinical Decision Support System works. The CDSS translates our machine learning model's predictions into a practical tool that clinicians can use during preoperative counseling. By entering a patient's clinical measurements, hormonal levels, and pathology results, clinicians get a probability estimate for successful sperm retrieval — helping both the medical team and the patient make more informed decisions about whether to proceed with micro-TESE surgery.

elements.label: CDSS Input Form
elements.script: This is the CDSS prediction form where you can enter patient clinical data. The input fields are organized by clinical relevance, starting with hormonal markers like LH, FSH, and Testosterone, then demographic features like Age and BMI, followed by ultrasound measurements. Each field shows its interquartile range as a placeholder — these are the typical values from our 2,413-patient cohort. Fields are color-coded based on the entered value: green for values within the normal interquartile range, and amber or red for values outside typical ranges. The bilateral pathology inputs allow you to specify right and left testis histology separately.

elements.label: Pathology Explanation Accordion
elements.script: This section explains how pathology is handled in the CDSS. In the original dataset, pathology was recorded as percentage patterns in testicular tissue — for example, SCO 45 percent, Maturation Arrest 30 percent. The CDSS maintains this structure by accepting right and left pathology inputs separately. These pathology values are integrated as a penalty or bonus term in the prediction logit, reflecting their strong influence on sperm retrieval outcomes. The prediction is aligned with CatBoost v2, the best-performing model with AUC 0.8306.

elements.label: FAQ Accordion
elements.script: This frequently asked questions section is designed for universities and treatment centers that might adopt this CDSS tool. It addresses common questions about how pathology is processed, the validation methodology behind the predictions, and the model's limitations. The key takeaway is that this CDSS uses a rigorously validated CatBoost model from the v2 pipeline — with proper cross-validation, bootstrap confidence intervals, and calibration analysis — making it suitable for research and clinical pilot studies, though further external validation on independent cohorts would strengthen the evidence before widespread clinical deployment.

elements.label: References Stat Card
elements.script: The literature review is anchored by 41 key references spanning reproductive medicine, machine learning in healthcare, and clinical prediction modeling. These references were systematically selected to cover the theoretical foundations, methodological precedents, and clinical context for predicting sperm retrieval outcomes in non-obstructive azoospermia.

elements.label: Research Gaps Stat Card
elements.script: Seven specific research gaps were identified through the literature review, and each gap is directly addressed by this study. These gaps include the lack of large-cohort ML studies for NOA prediction, insufficient use of histopathology data as predictive features, absence of explainability in existing models, and the need for properly validated clinical decision support tools in reproductive medicine.

elements.label: Research Gaps List
elements.script: This list details the seven articulated research gaps and how each one is addressed by our study. Identifying and explicitly addressing research gaps is essential for a PhD dissertation because it demonstrates the study's contribution to the field. Each gap maps to specific methodological choices — for example, the gap about small sample sizes motivated our use of the large 2,413-patient Royan cohort, and the gap about lack of histopathology integration led to our novel bilateral pathology feature engineering.

elements.label: Bibliography List
elements.script: This searchable bibliography contains all 41 anchor citations used in the study. Each reference includes authors, year, title, source journal, and a relevance note explaining why it's included. You can search by author name, keyword, or year to quickly find specific references. These citations support everything from the clinical background of NOA and micro-TESE, to the machine learning methodologies used, to the statistical validation approaches employed.

elements.label: Theoretical Frameworks List
elements.script: Nine theoretical frameworks underpin this research, providing the conceptual scaffolding for our methodology. These frameworks span machine learning theory, clinical prediction model development standards like TRIPOD, explainable AI principles, and medical informatics best practices. Having a strong theoretical foundation is important for demonstrating that the research methodology is grounded in established scientific principles rather than ad hoc decisions.

elements.label: Key Benchmark Studies
elements.script: These benchmark studies represent the most relevant prior work against which our study is compared. They include previous attempts at predicting sperm retrieval outcomes using various approaches — from simple logistic regression to more recent machine learning methods. Our study advances beyond these benchmarks in several ways: larger cohort size, more comprehensive feature engineering with bilateral pathology, rigorous nested cross-validation, and the addition of explainability through SHAP analysis.

elements.label: Final Deliverable Stat Card
elements.script: The final deliverable of this entire pipeline is the PhD dissertation — a comprehensive document integrating all six agent phases from data collection through preprocessing, modeling, validation, and explainability analysis. The dissertation represents the culmination of the research and is designed to meet the requirements for a doctoral degree in Medical Informatics at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences.

elements.label: Dataset Stat Card
elements.script: The analytical cohort of 2,413 patients from the Royan Institute forms the data foundation for the entire study. This is one of the largest single-center cohorts used for sperm retrieval prediction in published literature, providing robust statistical power for training and validating multiple machine learning models.

elements.label: Agent 6 Production List
elements.script: This list summarizes what the integration agent produced — it's essentially a quality control checklist showing how all the pieces from the previous five pipeline phases were assembled. The integration process involved synchronizing all dashboard narratives to the authoritative cohort, aligning metrics to the finalized v2 pipeline, incorporating the pathology block summary, generating the integration report, and validating consistency across all pages. This systematic integration ensures that no contradictory information appears anywhere in the dashboard.

elements.label: Data-to-Dissertation Chain
elements.script: This visual chain shows the complete journey from raw data to PhD dissertation in a single flow. Starting from the Royan workbook — the original clinical database — through the 2,413 patients, 73 engineered features, 18 pathology features, 16 tested models narrowed to 5, nested cross-validation with calibration and Decision Curve Analysis, arriving at CatBoost with AUC 0.8306 and 95 percent confidence interval 0.823 to 0.845. This end-to-end traceability is important for research reproducibility and for demonstrating the complete methodology during defense presentations.

article-a-overview: This paper by Jamalirad, Sabbaghian, and Vakili Arki presents what is, to the authors' knowledge, the largest pure machine learning study for predicting sperm retrieval success in microdissection testicular sperm extraction, commonly known as micro-TESE. The study draws on two thousand four hundred and thirteen consecutive patients with non-obstructive azoospermia treated at the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran, between two thousand seven and twenty twenty-two. The authors constructed a seventy-three-feature analytic matrix by augmenting standard clinical variables with eighteen newly engineered features derived from a custom natural language processing pipeline applied to free-text bilateral pathology reports. They benchmarked sixteen machine learning algorithms under a leakage-controlled five-by-five nested cross-validation design. The champion model, CatBoost, achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of zero point eight three zero six, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. The study is notable for its transparent disclosure of a version one pipeline data-leakage incident and its subsequent remediation, its adherence to TRIPOD plus AI twenty twenty-four and PROBAST plus AI twenty twenty-five reporting standards, and its SHAP-based interpretability analysis identifying luteinizing hormone, age, follicle-stimulating hormone, and testosterone as the dominant predictors. The paper targets Computers in Biology and Medicine and represents a significant methodological advance in reproductive medicine prediction modeling.
article-a-abstract: The abstract of this paper lays out a comprehensive roadmap for the study. It opens by establishing the clinical problem: micro-TESE is the standard of care for sperm retrieval in non-obstructive azoospermia, but success rates range from thirty to sixty percent and are notoriously difficult to predict from any single clinical feature. The authors note that existing machine learning predictors have been limited by small single-center cohorts, typically under five hundred patients, narrow algorithm comparisons, inconsistent leakage controls, and minimal integration of free-text pathology information. The methods section of the abstract describes the analysis of two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients, with a custom NLP pipeline parsing bilateral pathology reports into eighteen engineered features, expanding fifty-five raw variables to a seventy-three-feature matrix. Sixteen models were trained using nested cross-validation with RobustScaler standardization, k-nearest-neighbor imputation, missingness-indicator encoding, and SMOTE applied strictly within cross-validation folds. The results highlight CatBoost as the champion with an AUC of zero point eight three zero six, confidence interval zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five, followed by XGBoost at zero point eight two one four and LightGBM at zero point eight two one three. At the Youden-optimal threshold of zero point four four four to zero point five zero seven, accuracy reached seventy-six point nine three percent, sensitivity sixty-seven point eight four percent, and specificity eighty-two point two zero percent. SHAP analysis identified luteinizing hormone, age, FSH, and testosterone as the top four predictors. The abstract concludes by noting this is the largest pure ML benchmark for micro-TESE prediction to date.
article-a-key-results: Let me walk you through the headline findings of this study. In a cohort of two thousand four hundred and thirteen men with non-obstructive azoospermia undergoing micro-TESE, the overall sperm retrieval success rate was thirty-six point seven percent, meaning roughly one in three patients achieved successful retrieval. The CatBoost classifier, trained on seventy-three features including eighteen NLP-derived bilateral pathology variables, emerged as the champion model with an AUC of zero point eight three zero six and a tight ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. This outperformed fifteen alternative algorithms in the leakage-controlled five-by-five nested cross-validation framework. At the Youden-optimal threshold, which ranged from zero point four four four to zero point five zero seven across resamples, the model achieved accuracy of zero point seven seven, sensitivity of zero point six eight, specificity of zero point eight two, an F-one score of zero point six eight, and a Brier score of zero point two one three. The decision curve analysis confirmed superior net benefit across the clinically relevant threshold range of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. SHAP feature importance analysis revealed that the endocrine axis dominates prediction: luteinizing hormone had the highest mean absolute SHAP value at zero point three one seven, followed by age at zero point two seven zero, FSH at zero point two five zero, and testosterone at zero point two one two. These findings are consistent with the known pathophysiology of testicular failure and support the model's biological face validity.
article-a-introduction: The introduction builds a compelling case for why this study was needed. It begins by establishing that non-obstructive azoospermia affects approximately ten to fifteen percent of azoospermic men and roughly one percent of the general male population. For these patients, micro-TESE followed by intracytoplasmic sperm injection remains the only reliable pathway to biological fatherhood, yet the procedure carries a roughly coin-flip success probability of thirty to sixty percent. The authors then survey the predictor landscape, noting that hormonal markers like FSH, LH, and testosterone, along with patient age, anthropometry, testicular volume, karyotype abnormalities, and Y-chromosome microdeletions have all been investigated but no single variable or simple score has achieved sufficient discrimination for clinical translation. The machine learning literature is reviewed systematically. Early work by Zeadna and colleagues on one hundred nineteen patients showed gradient-boosted trees could match logistic regression. Bachelot et al. achieved an AUC of zero point nine zero zero on two hundred one patients but with only twenty-six prospective test cases. Kobayashi et al. used AutoML on four hundred fifty patients with AUC zero point seven two five. The authors identify four persistent limitations in the field: small sample sizes, narrow algorithm comparisons, inconsistent leakage controls, and failure to systematically engineer free-text histopathology into structured features. They cite the scoping review by Jamalirad et al. and the twenty twenty-six state-of-the-art review by Kandil et al. as both flagging these exact gaps. The introduction concludes by articulating five specific objectives, including constructing the seventy-three-feature matrix, benchmarking sixteen algorithms, and transparently reporting a version one leakage incident.
article-a-methods: The methods section is organized into six subsections covering the full analytical pipeline. Section two point one describes the study design and population, detailing the retrospective single-center cohort from the Royan Institute spanning two thousand seven to twenty twenty-two, with two thousand four hundred and thirteen analytical patients after excluding thirty-seven records with missing primary outcomes. Section two point two addresses ethics and data governance, including IRB approval and the computational infrastructure used. Section two point three presents the feature engineering and NLP pipeline, which is arguably the most novel methodological contribution, describing how free-text bilateral pathology reports were parsed into sixteen quantitative histopathological indicators plus two cohort metadata flags. Section two point four details the machine learning pipeline itself, including the sixteen-model benchmark, the five-by-five nested cross-validation design, and the strict within-fold preprocessing discipline. Section two point five covers the statistical analysis framework for discrimination, calibration, decision curve analysis, and threshold optimization. Finally, section two point six describes adherence to TRIPOD plus AI twenty twenty-four and PROBAST plus AI twenty twenty-five reporting standards. Together, these subsections present one of the most methodologically rigorous machine learning pipelines in the reproductive medicine prediction literature, with particular attention to leakage prevention and transparent reporting.
article-a-methods-sub-1: Section two point one describes the study design and population in detail. The cohort was assembled retrospectively from the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran, a high-volume national andrology referral center. It comprised consecutive men diagnosed with non-obstructive azoospermia who underwent micro-TESE between two thousand seven and twenty twenty-two. Eligibility required three criteria: a documented clinical diagnosis of NOA established by repeated semen analyses, hormonal profile, and physical examination; completion of at least one micro-TESE procedure with operative documentation; and availability of the binary primary outcome, defined as successful sperm retrieval of at least one motile or immotile spermatozoon identified by the embryologist on intra-operative wet preparation. Salvage attempts on patients with prior failed retrieval were captured via a Salvage Count integer and an is Primary binary flag. Of two thousand four hundred and fifty records screened from the source workbook, thirty-seven patients lacked a valid outcome entry and were excluded, yielding two thousand four hundred and thirteen analytical patients. The cohort comprised eight hundred eighty-six patients with successful retrieval, representing thirty-six point seven percent, and one thousand five hundred twenty-seven with failed retrieval at sixty-three point three percent, giving an approximate one point seven two to one failure-to-success class ratio. Primary micro-TESE attempts represented ninety-one point two percent of the cohort at two thousand two hundred thirty-four cases, while salvage attempts accounted for eight point eight percent at two hundred sixteen cases.
article-a-methods-sub-2: Section two point two covers ethics and data governance. The study received approval as a retrospective de-identified cohort from the Royan Institute Institutional Review Board, with a waiver of individual consent given the retrospective design and absence of any patient contact. All variables were extracted from the institutional electronic medical record and converted into a fully de-identified analytical dataset prior to any modeling step. Direct identifiers, specifically the ID and Roy-ID fields, were retained only as opaque keys to support quality assurance and were never used as model inputs. This is an important methodological safeguard because inadvertent inclusion of patient identifiers can create spurious predictive signal. All computation, including NLP feature extraction, model training, calibration, and SHAP analysis, was performed on a secure GPU workstation equipped with an NVIDIA H one hundred with eighty gigabytes of memory, an AMD EPYC ninety-six fifty-four ninety-six-core processor, and two hundred fifty-six gigabytes of DDR five RAM. The hardware specification is relevant because it enabled the computationally intensive five-by-five nested cross-validation with fifty iterations of randomized hyperparameter search across sixteen models, a design that would be prohibitively slow on consumer-grade hardware.
article-a-methods-sub-3: Section two point three describes the feature engineering and NLP pipeline, which is the study's most distinctive methodological contribution. The raw workbook contained fifty-five columns. A structural audit identified nine columns with one hundred percent missingness, including Cancer Type, Cancer Treatment, SHBG, TSH, inhibin B, and AMH, which were removed. Forty additional variables exceeded fifty percent missingness but were retained when clinically meaningful and complemented by explicit binary missingness-indicator features. The core innovation is the custom NLP pipeline applied to two free-text bilateral pathology fields, Pathology RT for the right testis and Pathology LT for the left testis. The pipeline used regular-expression patterns tuned on a manual gold standard to extract sixteen quantitative histopathological indicators, eight per testis side, covering Sertoli-cell-only pattern, complete spermatogenesis, maturation arrest at four sequential developmental phases, hypospermatogenesis, and severe hypospermatogenesis. A biologically motivated hierarchy rule ensured that when arrest was indicated at an earlier developmental phase, later-phase indicators were set to zero. Two additional cohort metadata flags, is Primary and Salvage Count, brought the total to eighteen engineered features, expanding the matrix to seventy-three columns. Pipeline validation achieved over ninety-five percent concordance with a pathologist-annotated gold standard across all sixteen indicators, with eight reaching one hundred percent concordance. The prevalence of individual diagnostic indicators ranged from seven percent for severe hypospermatogenesis to forty-one percent for Sertoli-cell-only patterns, matching expected distributions in a national referral cohort.
article-a-methods-sub-4: Section two point four details the machine learning pipeline, which implements a leakage-controlled supervised learning framework in Python using scikit-learn, CatBoost, LightGBM, XGBoost, and imbalanced-learn. The analytical cohort of two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients was partitioned by an outer stratified eighty-twenty split with random state forty-two into a training set of one thousand nine hundred thirty patients and a held-out test set of four hundred eighty-three patients. An alternative seventy-thirty split was retained for calibration analyses. Within the training set, a nested cross-validation design was employed: an outer loop produced unbiased generalization estimates while an inner five-fold loop performed RandomizedSearchCV hyperparameter tuning with fifty iterations per model. Critically, all preprocessing steps, including k-nearest-neighbor imputation, missingness-indicator construction, RobustScaler standardization, categorical encoding, maturation arrest hierarchy enforcement, and SMOTE oversampling, were fitted strictly within each training fold and applied to the corresponding held-out fold. This discipline was applied in the corrected version two pipeline after a version one outlier-clipping bug was identified. Sixteen candidate algorithms were benchmarked, spanning regularized linear models, kernel methods, tree ensembles, and a neural network. A feature-selection union combining Mann-Whitney U screening, mutual-information ranking, and recursive feature elimination yielded a fifty-one-feature subset for sensitivity analyses.
article-a-methods-sub-5: Section two point five presents the statistical analysis framework for discrimination, calibration, decision curve analysis, and threshold optimization. Discrimination was quantified by the area under the ROC curve, with ninety-five percent confidence intervals obtained from one thousand bootstrap resamples of the held-out test set. Pairwise differences between models were reported with Benjamini-Hochberg multiple-comparison correction across the sixteen-model grid. At each model's Youden-optimal threshold, defined as the point maximizing the sum of sensitivity and specificity minus one, the authors reported accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, F-one score, and Matthews correlation coefficient. The Youden threshold range across resamples was zero point four four four to zero point five zero seven, consistent with the cohort base rate of thirty-six point seven percent. Calibration was assessed using the Brier score and decile-binned reliability diagrams, with Platt scaling and isotonic regression available as fallback recalibration options. Clinical utility was evaluated through decision curve analysis, comparing net benefit of the model-guided strategy against treat-all and treat-none reference strategies. Three operating points were considered: the Youden-optimal threshold as the default, a sensitivity-prioritized threshold requiring sensitivity of at least zero point eight five, and a specificity-prioritized threshold requiring specificity of at least zero point nine zero. Global feature importance was estimated with TreeSHAP for the CatBoost champion, while complementary multi-method interpretability including LIME, partial dependence, and accumulated local effects plots is detailed in a companion paper.
article-a-methods-sub-6: Section two point six describes the study's adherence to reporting standards and risk-of-bias assessment. The study was prospectively designed to comply with the TRIPOD plus AI twenty twenty-four reporting checklist, which is the updated guidance for reporting clinical prediction models that use regression or machine learning methods, published by Collins, Moons, Dhiman, and colleagues in the BMJ. Risk of bias was assessed using both the original PROBAST tool, developed by Wolff, Moons, Riley, and colleagues, and the updated PROBAST plus AI twenty twenty-five framework published by Moons, Damen, Kaul, and colleagues. The assessment covered four domains: Participants, Predictors, Outcome, and Analysis. Importantly, both the version one and version two pipelines were graded separately to make the remediation auditable. This dual grading is a notable transparency measure that allows readers and reviewers to see exactly how the risk-of-bias profile changed after the leakage correction. Item-level adherence and domain-level judgments are summarized in the accompanying TRIPOD checklist and PROBAST assessment structures. The supplementary materials provide hyperparameter distributions, fold-best parameters, reliability diagram coordinates, decision curve analysis numerical arrays, and the SHAP summary table, along with a versioned data and code release for reproducibility.
article-a-results: The results section is organized into six subsections that systematically build the evidence for the study's conclusions. Section three point one characterizes the analytical cohort and its demographics, establishing the baseline for all subsequent analyses. Section three point two presents the sixteen-model performance comparison, revealing the dominance of gradient-boosted tree ensembles. Section three point three provides a deep dive into the champion CatBoost model's operating-point performance. Section three point four examines calibration through Brier scores and reliability diagrams, and clinical utility through decision curve analysis. Section three point five reports the SHAP-based feature importance analysis, connecting the model's behavior to known reproductive endocrinology. Finally, section three point six provides a transparent disclosure of the version one to version two pipeline correction, including the specific bug that was identified and how it was remediated. This structure moves logically from cohort description through model selection, champion characterization, calibration validation, interpretability, and methodological transparency, providing a comprehensive evidence package for the study's claims.
article-a-results-sub-1: Section three point one describes the cohort characteristics and demographics. The analytical cohort comprised two thousand four hundred and thirteen men with non-obstructive azoospermia, of whom eight hundred eighty-six, or thirty-six point seven percent, achieved successful sperm retrieval, while one thousand five hundred twenty-seven, or sixty-three point three percent, did not. This yields a class-imbalance ratio of approximately one point seven two to one. Primary micro-TESE attempts constituted ninety-one point two percent of the cohort at two thousand two hundred thirty-four cases, with salvage procedures accounting for eight point eight percent at two hundred sixteen cases. The eighty-twenty stratified split produced one thousand nine hundred thirty training and four hundred eighty-three held-out test patients. Variable-level missingness was notably heterogeneous: demographic variables like age and race had zero percent missingness, while FSH was missing in twenty-two point two percent, LH in twenty-five point one percent, testosterone in twenty-two point six percent, and Pathology LT in forty-nine percent of cases. Anthropometric measures were missing in sixty-four to sixty-seven percent, karyotype in sixty-two point three percent, and sonographic testicular size in eighty-two to eighty-three percent. Diabetes and hypertension were missing in over ninety-nine percent of records. The eighteen engineered features from the NLP pipeline brought the final analytic matrix to seventy-three columns, with outcome label and predictor distributions preserved across the split by stratified sampling.
article-a-results-sub-2: Section three point two presents the sixteen-model performance comparison from the corrected version two pipeline. Gradient-boosted tree ensembles dominated the top of the ranking. CatBoost emerged as the version two champion with an AUC of zero point eight three zero six and a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. XGBoost followed closely at zero point eight two one four with confidence interval zero point eight one four to zero point eight three five, then LightGBM at zero point eight two one three with confidence interval zero point eight zero nine to zero point eight three six, and Random Forest at zero point eight one five seven with confidence interval zero point eight zero nine to zero point eight two nine. Logistic Regression in the version two pipeline produced AUC zero point seven nine two six. The middle tier included Stacking Ensemble at zero point seven two two, Voting Ensemble at zero point seven zero six, and MLP neural network at zero point six seven zero. The bottom tier comprised Ridge Classifier at zero point six six eight, K-Nearest Neighbors at zero point six five two, Decision Tree at zero point six four six, Elastic Net and Lasso at approximately zero point five nine five, SVM with RBF kernel at zero point five four seven, and Naive Bayes at zero point five two three. Pairwise AUC comparisons between the top three models were not statistically significant after Benjamini-Hochberg correction, indicating robustness within the tree-ensemble class. Overfitting gaps were moderate for CatBoost at zero point one eight nine and LightGBM at zero point one seven zero, but higher for several other models.
article-a-results-sub-3: Section three point three provides a detailed analysis of the champion CatBoost version two model. CatBoost was retained as champion based on its leading discrimination, balanced operating-point performance, robust calibration, and compatibility with mixed categorical and numeric features. At the Youden-optimal threshold band of zero point four four four to zero point five zero seven, the model achieved accuracy of zero point seven six nine three, or seventy-six point nine three percent. Sensitivity was zero point six seven eight four, meaning the model correctly identified about sixty-eight percent of true sperm-retrieval successes. Specificity was zero point eight two two zero, correctly identifying about eighty-two percent of true failures. Positive predictive value was zero point six eight eight three, meaning that when the model predicted success, approximately sixty-nine percent of those patients actually retrieved sperm. Negative predictive value was zero point eight one five two, meaning that when the model predicted failure, approximately eighty-two percent of those patients did indeed fail. The F-one score was zero point six eight three two and the Matthews correlation coefficient was zero point five zero one nine, indicating moderate-to-strong agreement above chance under class imbalance. The Brier score was zero point two one three. The selected hyperparameters were depth six, six hundred fifty-seven iterations, L-two leaf regularization of three point five one, learning rate of zero point zero zero six one seven, and RobustScaler for continuous predictors. The authors note that the operating point favors specificity slightly over sensitivity, which they view as clinically appropriate for a counseling-oriented preoperative tool.
article-a-results-sub-4: Section three point four examines calibration and clinical utility. Calibration was strongest for LightGBM with a Brier score of zero point two zero one, followed by Gradient Boosting at zero point two zero two, XGBoost at zero point two zero six, CatBoost at zero point two one three, and Stacking Ensemble at zero point two two six. The decile-binned reliability diagrams for CatBoost showed close adherence to the ideal y-equals-x diagonal across the second through ninth deciles. Specific calibration points included an observed fraction of zero point two five two at a predicted value of zero point one five one, zero point three four six at predicted zero point four five three, zero point four six two at predicted zero point five four five, and zero point seven four seven at predicted zero point eight five zero. Only the highest decile bin showed modest under-confidence, with an observed fraction of zero point seven five four at a predicted value of zero point nine four one. Decision curve analysis confirmed positive incremental net benefit of the model-guided strategy over both treat-all and treat-none reference strategies across the clinically relevant threshold window of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. Within this window, the model yields meaningful net interventions avoided per one hundred patients while preserving identification of patients with high retrieval probability. This is exactly the operating regime in which preoperative counseling decisions are made, supporting the model's clinical utility for shared decision-making.
article-a-results-sub-5: Section three point five reports the SHAP-based feature importance analysis, which provides critical interpretability for the CatBoost version two champion model. Global SHAP values identified the endocrine axis as the dominant predictive signal. Luteinizing hormone ranked first with a mean absolute SHAP value of zero point three one seven, followed by age at zero point two seven zero, follicle-stimulating hormone at zero point two five zero, and testosterone at zero point two one two. This ranking is consistent with the endocrine pathophysiology of testicular failure and aligns with prior literature by Kim and colleagues and Siva and colleagues highlighting the testosterone-to-LH and testosterone-to-FSH ratios as predictors of spermatogenic reserve. The next tier comprised anthropometric and testicular-volume descriptors: body weight at zero point one seven four, Sakamoto left testicular volume at zero point one seven one, BMI at zero point one six nine, height at zero point one six six, right testicular sonographic volume at zero point one five six, and estimated left testicular volume at zero point one two nine. Seminal plasma pH contributed zero point one two eight. Notably, in the corrected version two pipeline, the aggregate bilateral pathology features and most lifestyle and comorbidity flags ranked at or near zero in global SHAP importance. The authors interpret this not as pathology being uninformative, but rather that hormonal and anthropometric variables dominate the global signal once the pathology block is properly handled without the version one clipping artifact. Partner age was deliberately excluded on biological grounds, as male testicular sperm retrieval has no mechanism by which the female partner's age could affect it.
article-a-results-sub-6: Section three point six provides a transparent disclosure of the version one to version two pipeline remediation, which is a remarkable act of scientific transparency. The original version one nested cross-validation grid placed LightGBM first with an AUC of zero point seven three two seven plus or minus zero point zero zero five seven. Post-hoc audit revealed that an interquartile-range clipping step, intended to dampen outliers in hormonal measurements, had also been applied to the engineered pathology features. Because these features were heavily right-skewed with a substantial fraction of zeros, the global IQR bounds collapsed the long tail and effectively zeroed out the histopathological signal in many records. This constituted a subtle form of pathology-feature data leakage. Two additional issues were corrected in parallel: documentation incorrectly stated that Z-score standardization had been used when hyperparameter logs confirmed RobustScaler was actually selected, and Partner Age was retained despite being biologically implausible as a determinant of male sperm retrieval. The version two pipeline removed the IQR-clipping step, corrected the scaling documentation, excluded Partner Age, and re-ran the entire sixteen-model benchmark with strictly within-fold preprocessing. The corrected leaderboard places CatBoost first at AUC zero point eight three zero six. Interestingly, the leakage had paradoxically suppressed rather than inflated discrimination, because clipping had collapsed rather than separated feature variance. The authors retained version one results in the supplementary appendix for reference, demonstrating exemplary adherence to TRIPOD plus AI and PROBAST plus AI transparency expectations.
article-a-discussion: The discussion section contextualizes the findings within the broader literature and addresses clinical implications and limitations. The authors identify three distinguishing features of their study. First, the cohort of two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients is the largest pure machine learning micro-TESE prediction cohort reported to date. The next-largest dedicated ML study by Bachelot et al. was an order of magnitude smaller at two hundred one patients. The only published cohort exceeding one thousand patients is the Ceyhan multi-center nomogram at three thousand ninety-three patients, which is a clinical regression nomogram rather than an algorithmically diverse ML benchmark. Second, the sixteen-model benchmark is the most comprehensive algorithm comparison in this domain. Third, the integration of NLP-derived bilateral pathology features within a leakage-controlled pipeline is the first such integration in published micro-TESE prediction work. The authors carefully compare their AUC of zero point eight three zero six against competitors, noting that Li et al. reported zero point nine five zero but on only one hundred sixty-seven patients with high overfitting risk, and Wang et al. achieved zero point eight three nine but required invasive intra-operative pathology. Clinical implications center on two uses: preoperative counseling to set realistic expectations, and triage in resource-constrained settings. The authors explicitly caution against using the model as a stand-alone exclusion criterion. Limitations include single-center design, absence of external validation, unavailability of AMH and inhibin B, and the binary outcome not capturing downstream ICSI or live-birth rates. The discussion also provides a generalizable lesson about how IQR-based outlier handling can corrupt feature engineering in clinical prediction models.
article-a-conclusions: The conclusions section distills the study's contributions into three key messages. First, the empirical result: in a retrospective single-center cohort of two thousand four hundred and thirteen men with non-obstructive azoospermia, a CatBoost classifier trained on a seventy-three-feature matrix including eighteen NLP-derived bilateral pathology and cohort features achieved an AUC of zero point eight three zero six with a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. This outperformed fifteen alternative algorithms in a leakage-controlled five-by-five nested cross-validation. At the Youden-optimal threshold, accuracy was zero point seven seven, sensitivity zero point six eight, specificity zero point eight two, F-one zero point six eight, and Brier zero point two one three. Decision curve analysis confirmed superior net benefit across the zero point zero five to zero point six zero threshold range, and SHAP identified LH, age, FSH, and testosterone as the dominant predictors. Second, three methodological contributions: a transparent version one to version two pipeline remediation demonstrating TRIPOD plus AI and PROBAST plus AI transparency in practice; a sixteen-model benchmark exceeding the algorithm-class diversity of any prior study; and a deterministic NLP pipeline converting free-text bilateral pathology reports into sixteen quantitative features. Third, the authors support using the CatBoost model as a preoperative counseling and shared decision-making aid, with the explicit caveat that prospective and multi-center external validation remains a priority before deployment-stage clinical use.
article-a-references: The reference list of this paper spans forty-two citations and reflects the interdisciplinary nature of the work. Several key references deserve particular attention. Kandil et al., reference one, published in the World Journal of Men's Health in twenty twenty-six, provides the state-of-the-art review of artificial intelligence in non-obstructive azoospermia that frames the field's current gaps. Jamalirad et al., reference two, published in Human Reproduction Open in twenty twenty-five, is the systematic scoping review by the lead author that identified the methodological limitations this study aims to address. The foundational machine learning comparator studies include Zeadna et al. in Human Reproduction twenty twenty with one hundred nineteen patients, Bachelot et al. in JMIR twenty twenty-three with two hundred one patients, and Kobayashi et al. in Andrologia twenty twenty-three with four hundred fifty patients. The TRIPOD plus AI twenty twenty-four guidelines by Collins, Moons, and Dhiman, reference seventeen, and the PROBAST plus AI twenty twenty-five framework by Moons, Damen, and Kaul, reference eighteen, provide the reporting and risk-of-bias standards the study follows. Methodological foundations include Prokhorenkova et al.'s CatBoost paper from NeurIPS twenty eighteen, Lundberg and Lee's SHAP framework from NeurIPS twenty seventeen, and Vickers and Elkin's decision curve analysis from Medical Decision Making twenty zero six. The NLP literature is represented by recent twenty twenty-six publications on pathology report parsing and large language model-assisted normalization. Reference forty-two points to the companion paper on the integrated explainability and clinical decision support framework.
article-a-supplements: The supplementary materials for this paper comprise five tables that provide the detailed technical documentation necessary for reproducibility and methodological scrutiny. Supplementary Table S one contains the full data dictionary and missingness inventory, documenting every variable's description, units, observed range, missingness percentage, and post-cleaning retention status. Supplementary Table S two provides the hyperparameter search spaces and fold-best parameters for all sixteen models in the benchmark. Supplementary Table S three contains the Brier scores, reliability diagram coordinates, and decision curve analysis numerical arrays that underpin the calibration and clinical utility figures. Supplementary Table S four presents the complete SHAP global summary with mean absolute SHAP values for every feature in the CatBoost version two champion model. Finally, Supplementary Table S five provides the pipeline remediation audit trail and reproducibility manifest, documenting the chronological sequence of the version one to version two correction. Together, these supplements enable independent verification of every claim in the main text and provide the raw numerical data behind all figures and performance metrics.
article-a-supplements-sub-1: Supplementary Table S one provides the full data dictionary and missingness inventory for the study. This table documents every variable in the analytical matrix with its description, units, observed range, missingness percentage, post-cleaning retention status, and post-engineering feature lineage. Variables are categorized into four retention statuses: retained, retained with missingness indicator, excluded due to one hundred percent missingness, and excluded on biological grounds. The nine variables excluded for complete missingness include Cancer Type, Cancer Treatment, Surgery Therapeutic, Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin, TSH, inhibin B, AMH, Infertility length, and Torsion of spermatic cord. Partner Age was excluded on biological grounds as it has no mechanism to affect male testicular sperm retrieval. Forty variables with missingness exceeding fifty percent were retained with explicit missingness indicators, including Karyotype at sixty-two point three percent missing, Y-chromosome microdeletion at sixty-two point four percent, sonographic testicular size at eighty-two to eighty-three percent, Diabetes at ninety-nine point one percent, and Hypertension at ninety-nine point eight percent. The eighteen NLP-derived variables are enumerated: SCO and CSTH for right and left testes, maturation arrest at four developmental phases for each side, hypospermatogenesis and severe hypospermatogenesis for each side, plus the is Primary and Salvage Count cohort flags. This table is essential for anyone attempting to replicate the feature engineering pipeline.
article-a-supplements-sub-2: Supplementary Table S two documents the hyperparameter search spaces and fold-best parameters for all sixteen models in the benchmark. Each model's search space was explored using RandomizedSearchCV with fifty iterations per model in the inner five-fold cross-validation loop. The table provides specific examples: Logistic Regression searched over C values in a log-uniform distribution from ten to the negative four to ten squared, class weight options of none or balanced, the liblinear solver, and scaler choices between RobustScaler and StandardScaler. LightGBM searched over number of leaves from fifteen to two hundred fifty-five, learning rate in log-uniform from ten to the negative three to zero point three, number of estimators from two hundred to one thousand five hundred, minimum child samples from five to eighty, and regularization lambda in log-uniform from ten to the negative three to ten. The CatBoost champion was ultimately selected with depth six, six hundred fifty-seven iterations, L-two leaf regularization of three point five one, learning rate of zero point zero zero six one seven, and RobustScaler. This level of hyperparameter documentation is explicitly required by TRIPOD plus AI twenty twenty-four and enables independent reproduction of the model training process.
article-a-supplements-sub-3: Supplementary Table S three provides the numerical data underlying the calibration and decision curve analysis figures. It contains per-decile pairs of mean predicted value and observed fraction positive for the five models with reported Brier scores: LightGBM at zero point two zero one, Gradient Boosting at zero point two zero two, XGBoost at zero point two zero six, CatBoost at zero point two one three, and Stacking Ensemble at zero point two two six. The CatBoost reliability points are explicitly listed: zero point zero four five predicted with zero point one seven three observed, zero point one five one predicted with zero point two five two observed, zero point two six one predicted with zero point one nine three observed, zero point three five three predicted with zero point two six six observed, zero point four five three predicted with zero point three four six observed, zero point five four five predicted with zero point four six two observed, zero point six four one predicted with zero point five seven one observed, zero point seven four seven predicted with zero point six zero two observed, zero point eight five zero predicted with zero point seven four seven observed, and zero point nine four one predicted with zero point seven five four observed. The table also contains the decision curve analysis numerical arrays of threshold and net benefit pairs for the model-guided strategy and treat-all reference, plus the derived net interventions avoided per one hundred patients.
article-a-supplements-sub-4: Supplementary Table S four presents the complete SHAP global summary for the CatBoost version two champion model, ranking every feature by its mean absolute SHAP value across the analytical cohort. The top sixteen features are: luteinizing hormone at zero point three one seven, age at zero point two seven zero, follicle-stimulating hormone at zero point two five zero, testosterone at zero point two one two, body weight at zero point one seven four, Sakamoto left testicular volume at zero point one seven one, BMI at zero point one six nine, height at zero point one six six, right testicular sonographic volume at zero point one five six, estimated left testicular volume at zero point one two nine, seminal plasma pH at zero point one two eight, estimated right testicular volume at zero point one one one, left testicular sonographic volume at zero point zero eight five, estradiol at zero point zero four three, Sakamoto right testicular volume at zero point zero three nine, and prolactin at zero point zero two zero. Features ranked seventeenth through twenty-ninth, including Pathology RT, Pathology LT, race, infertile family members, habits, occupation with toxic exposure, diabetes, hypertension, surgical trauma, varicocele, testicular biopsy, inguinal hernia, and torsion of spermatic cord, all had global mean absolute SHAP values at or near zero in the preoperative framing. This distribution reflects the corrected version two feature importance after the version one IQR-clipping leakage was remediated.
article-a-supplements-sub-5: Supplementary Table S five provides the pipeline remediation audit trail and reproducibility manifest, which is a uniquely transparent documentation of the version one to version two correction process. The chronological audit log covers six key steps: first, detection of the IQR-clipping bug that zeroed pathology features in version one; second, correction of the methodology documentation from the incorrectly stated Z-score standardization to the actual RobustScaler used by hyperparameter tuning; third, exclusion of Partner Age on biological grounds; fourth, re-running the full sixteen-model nested cross-validation with strict within-fold preprocessing and in-fold SMOTE only; fifth, verification that the version two leaderboard, Brier scores, reliability arrays, decision curve analysis arrays, and SHAP table are mutually consistent; and sixth, deprecation of version one manuscript and pipeline summaries. The reproducibility manifest specifies Python three point ten or higher, scikit-learn, CatBoost, LightGBM, XGBoost, imbalanced-learn for SMOTE, and SHAP, with random state forty-two throughout. The pipeline was generated on April twenty-seventh, twenty twenty-six, on the NVIDIA H one hundred GPU workstation. The supplement also notes that a de-identified analytical dataset and version two model artifacts are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
article-a-tables: This paper contains six tables that collectively document the study's cohort, model performance, and methodological rigor. Table one presents the cohort characteristics and clinical features for all two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients, including the outcome distribution of eight hundred eighty-six successes and one thousand five hundred twenty-seven failures, the primary versus salvage attempt breakdown, the training and test set sizes, and the complete predictor inventory across hormonal, anthropometric, imaging, surgical, genetic, and histopathological domains. Table two is the sixteen-model comparison leaderboard from the corrected version two pipeline, ranking all algorithms by AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-one score, and Brier score, with CatBoost leading at zero point eight three zero six. Table three provides the detailed operating-point performance for the champion CatBoost model at the Youden-optimal threshold, including all nine performance metrics and the selected hyperparameters. Table four is a chronological competitor study comparison covering ten prior studies from Zeadna et al. in twenty twenty through Li et al. and Siva et al. in twenty twenty-six, documenting each study's cohort size, modeling approach, best AUC, validation strategy, and key limitations relative to the present study. Table five enumerates all eighteen NLP-engineered features with their side or scope, definition, and encoding scheme. Table six reports calibration via Brier scores and overfitting gaps across the top models, showing train AUC, cross-validated AUC, and the difference between them, with the Voting Ensemble showing the largest gap at zero point two nine four and LightGBM the smallest at zero point one seven zero.
article-a-figures: This paper includes six figures that visualize the study's key findings. Figure one is a CONSORT-style study flow diagram showing the progression from two thousand four hundred and fifty screened patients through exclusion of thirty-seven records to the two thousand four hundred and thirteen analytical cohort, with branching into outcome strata and attempt-type stratification, and the eighty-twenty and seventy-thirty data splits. Figure two presents overlay ROC curves for the top five models on the held-out test set, with CatBoost's curve dominating the upper-left region at AUC zero point eight three zero six, followed by XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest, and Logistic Regression, all plotted against the forty-five-degree chance reference line with bootstrap confidence-interval bands. Figure three shows multi-panel reliability diagrams for the top calibrated models, plotting mean predicted probability against observed fraction of positive outcomes in decile bins, with CatBoost tracking the ideal diagonal closely except for mild under-confidence at the highest decile. Figure four presents the decision curve analysis comparing CatBoost's net benefit against treat-all and treat-none strategies, demonstrating superior net benefit across the zero point zero five to zero point six zero threshold range, with vertical dashed lines marking the Youden-optimal threshold band. Figure five is a horizontal bar chart of global SHAP feature importance showing LH, age, FSH, and testosterone as the top four predictors. Figure six is a forest plot of cross-validated AUC across all sixteen algorithms, with tree ensembles clustering between zero point seven one and zero point eight three and the version two CatBoost champion rendered as a star marker at zero point eight three zero six.
article-a-tripod: The TRIPOD plus AI checklist for this paper demonstrates comprehensive adherence to the twenty twenty-four reporting guidelines published by Collins, Moons, Dhiman, and colleagues. The checklist covers twenty-two items plus three AI-specific items across Title, Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion, and Other sections. All items are marked as either Completed or Partial. The title correctly identifies the study as developing a multivariable prediction model using machine learning, mentions the target population and outcome. The abstract provides a structured summary with all required elements. The introduction explains clinical context, rationale, and specifies study objectives. Methods items are comprehensively addressed: source of data, participants, outcome definition, predictor specification, sample size justification with events per variable exceeding twenty, missing data handling with k-nearest-neighbor imputation and missingness indicators, statistical analysis methods, risk group creation via Youden's J threshold, and development versus validation comparison. The three AI-specific items are all completed: algorithm class disclosure with hyperparameter spaces and reproducibility via seed forty-two; data preprocessing steps fitted inside training folds only with an explicit leakage prevention plan; and explainability methods including SHAP and multi-method interpretation. Two items received Partial status: outcome blinding, because the retrospective design precludes formal blinding, and predictor blinding for the same reason. Results items including participant flow, model specification, and performance measures are all completed. The only other Partial item is validation, because external validation is pending and disclosed as a limitation.
article-a-probast: The PROBAST and PROBAST plus AI risk-of-bias assessment for this paper evaluates four domains: Participants, Predictors, Outcome, and Analysis. Each domain was assessed separately for both the version one and version two pipelines, which is an unusual and commendable transparency measure. The Participants domain received Low risk of bias in both versions, justified by the use of consecutive eligible NOA patients at a single high-volume referral center over a long accrual window from two thousand seven to twenty twenty-two, with only thirty-seven exclusions for missing primary outcome. The Predictors domain was rated Unclear in version one because pathology features were partly intra-operative, but Low in version two after the authors formally recognized pathology features as intra-operative or contemporaneous and discussed preoperative-only model variants. The Outcome domain received Low risk in both versions, as the binary surgical finding was recorded contemporaneously by the operating surgeon and verified against registry fields. The Analysis domain is where the most significant change occurred: it was rated High risk in version one due to the pathology-feature leakage from the IQR clipping bug, but Low risk in version two after the leakage was fixed, preprocessing was moved fully inside cross-validation folds, and the entire benchmark was re-run. The overall risk of bias is Low for the corrected version two pipeline but High for version one. Overall applicability concern is Low, as predictors and outcome match the intended preoperative counseling context, though the single-center Iranian referral cohort motivates prospective and multi-center external validation.
article-b-overview: This paper presents a multi-method explainable artificial intelligence framework for predicting sperm retrieval outcomes in microdissection testicular sperm extraction, commonly known as micro-TESE, integrated with a clinical decision support system designed for bedside use. The study, authored by Jamalirad, Sabbaghian, and Vakili Arki, analysed two thousand four hundred thirteen patients with non-obstructive azoospermia from the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran. The central model is a CatBoost classifier achieving an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. What distinguishes this work from prior micro-TESE prediction studies is not a marginal discrimination gain but rather its systematic approach to clinical translation. The authors apply three complementary explainability methods, SHapley Additive exPlanations or SHAP, Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations or LIME, and partial-dependence plots, demonstrating concordant feature rankings across all three.
