They further evaluate probability calibration via Brier scores and reliability diagrams, quantify clinical utility through decision curve analysis, and operationalise their findings in an eleven-feature clinical decision support system with explicit reference ranges, colour-coded validation logic, and a stateless web-deliverable architecture. The target journal is JMIR Medical Informatics, and the paper follows both the TRIPOD plus AI and PROBAST plus AI reporting frameworks.
article-b-abstract: The abstract concisely frames the study's four-part contribution. First, it establishes the clinical problem: micro-TESE fails in approximately two-thirds of non-obstructive azoospermia procedures, and existing machine learning models suffer from opacity, weak calibration, and absent bedside translation. Second, it describes the methods: two thousand four hundred thirteen patients, eight hundred eighty-six successful retrievals and one thousand five hundred twenty-seven failures, yielding a thirty-six point seven percent success rate. The frozen CatBoost classifier with an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six was interrogated with TreeSHAP, LIME, and one-way partial-dependence plots. Calibration was assessed via reliability diagrams and Brier scores for five top-ranking models, and clinical utility was quantified with decision curve analysis over threshold probabilities zero point zero one through zero point nine nine. Third, results: SHAP-based global importance was dominated by Luteinising Hormone with a mean absolute SHAP of zero point three one seven, Age at zero point two seven zero, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone at zero point two five zero, and Testosterone at zero point two one two. The top-four ranking was preserved across all three explainability methods. Decision curve analysis demonstrated net clinical benefit superior to both treat-all and treat-none strategies across thresholds of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. Finally, the conclusions state that multi-method explainable AI confirms a biologically coherent, method-stable predictor set, and that prospective multicentre validation is the necessary next step.
article-b-key-results: The key results summary distils the paper's most important quantitative findings into a single paragraph. The CatBoost model achieved an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six with a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five, and a Brier score of zero point two one three. In the cohort of two thousand four hundred thirteen non-obstructive azoospermia patients, the dominant predictors identified by SHAP were Luteinising Hormone with a mean absolute SHAP value of zero point three one seven, Age at zero point two seven zero, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone at zero point two five zero, and Testosterone at zero point two one two. These rankings were concordant across SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence analyses, a critical finding because it demonstrates that the importance hierarchy is not an artefact of any single explainability method. Decision curve analysis showed positive net clinical benefit for threshold probabilities of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero, meaning the model provides value for both risk-tolerant and risk-averse patients in the preoperative counselling window. All of this evidence is operationalised in an eleven-feature clinical decision support framework with explicit reference ranges and colour-coded validation logic, designed to translate opaque model outputs into actionable clinical guidance at the bedside.
article-b-introduction: The introduction establishes the clinical urgency and scientific gap that motivate this work. Non-obstructive azoospermia affects approximately one percent of all men and ten to fifteen percent of infertile men, and micro-TESE remains the principal surgical option. Yet sperm retrieval fails in roughly two-thirds of cases, imposing surgical, anaesthetic, and psychological burdens without reproductive benefit. The authors situate their contribution within a growing literature: Zeadna and colleagues developed an early gradient-boosting model on one hundred nineteen patients with an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight zero seven; Bachelot and colleagues benchmarked eight algorithms on two hundred one patients; Kobayashi and colleagues reported an AutoML system with an AUC of zero point seven two five; and several nomogram studies have scaled to multicentre settings, including work by Ceyhan and colleagues, Wang and colleagues, and Russo and colleagues. Critically, the authors argue that the field's current frontier is not marginal discrimination improvement but rather interpretability, calibration, and clinical translation, as identified in the twenty twenty-six World Journal of Men's Health review by Kandil and colleagues. The introduction explicitly states four research questions: which features drive the model and how stable are they across explainability methods; is the model adequately calibrated; across what threshold range does it deliver positive net clinical benefit; and which features should be exposed in a practical clinical decision support system. This structured framing gives the paper a clear evaluative scaffold.
article-b-methods: The Methods section describes a pre-specified explainability and clinical translation pipeline applied to the frozen version two CatBoost classifier from the companion methodological paper by Jamalirad, Sabbaghian, and Vakili Arki. Importantly, this paper does not develop a new model; it takes an existing champion model and subjects it to a rigorous multi-method interpretability analysis, calibration assessment, decision curve analysis, and clinical decision support system specification. The methods are organised into nine subsections covering study design and data source, predictors and preprocessing, model development reference, multi-method explainability using SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence plots, calibration assessment including Platt scaling, decision curve analysis following the framework of Vickers and Elkin, the clinical decision support system specification with eleven evidence-supported features, cross-method concordance metrics, and statistical reporting and software. All analyses follow the TRIPOD plus AI and PROBAST plus AI reporting frameworks, and all random seeds were fixed at forty-two for reproducibility. The analyses were performed in Python three point eleven using scikit-learn one point four, CatBoost one point two, SHAP zero point four five, and LIME zero point two.
article-b-methods-sub-1: The first methods subsection describes the study design and data source. The analytical cohort was assembled at the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran, for the companion methodological study. Eligible patients had confirmed non-obstructive azoospermia and underwent their first micro-TESE during the period two thousand seven through two thousand twenty-two. Patients with obstructive azoospermia or missing primary outcome were excluded. Of two thousand four hundred fifty records screened, two thousand four hundred thirteen were retained for analysis. The outcome variable, designated Outcome One, was defined as binary successful sperm retrieval, coded zero or one, at the first micro-TESE procedure. Eight hundred eighty-six patients, or thirty-six point seven percent, had a positive outcome, while one thousand five hundred twenty-seven patients, or sixty-three point three percent, experienced retrieval failure, yielding a failure-to-success ratio of approximately one point seven two to one. The study was Institutional Review Board approved and all data were de-identified. Two data splits were employed: a stratified eighty-twenty primary split with one thousand nine hundred thirty training and four hundred eighty-three test observations, and a seventy-thirty alternative split with one thousand six hundred eighty-nine training and seven hundred twenty-four test observations used specifically for calibration analyses.
article-b-methods-sub-2: The second methods subsection details the predictors and preprocessing pipeline. Twenty-nine candidate preoperative predictors were captured, spanning hormonal markers such as Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, Luteinising Hormone, Testosterone, Estradiol, Prolactin, Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone, Inhibin B, Anti-Mullerian Hormone, and Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin; anthropometric variables including Age, Height, Body Weight, and Body Mass Index; sonographic measurements of bilateral testicular volumes; seminal biochemistry, specifically seminal plasma pH; and pathology, demographic, and comorbidity variables including Karyotype and AZF microdeletions. Partner Age was explicitly excluded as biologically irrelevant to male testicular sperm retrieval and likely confounded with treatment urgency. Continuous variables were scaled with RobustScaler, selected via randomised hyperparameter search and fit on training folds only to prevent data leakage. Missing data were handled by k-nearest-neighbours imputation on continuous predictors after standardisation scaffolding, with explicit missingness indicators preserved as auxiliary binary features. Pathology free-text percentages were parsed by validated regular expressions with maturation-arrest hierarchy enforcement and bilateral consistency checks. Class imbalance was addressed by Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique, or SMOTE, applied only to training folds, which is the correct leakage-free approach.
article-b-methods-sub-3: The third methods subsection provides the model development context by referencing the companion paper. Sixteen candidate algorithms were benchmarked using a nested five-by-five cross-validation procedure with RandomizedSearchCV using fifty inner iterations per model and a fixed random state of forty-two. The algorithms ranged from LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and CatBoost through Random Forest, Voting Ensemble, Multi-Layer Perceptron, Ridge Classifier, K-Nearest Neighbours, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Elastic Net, and Lasso, to Radial Basis Function Support Vector Machine and Naive Bayes. Performance was characterised by Area Under the Curve, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-one score, and Brier score. The version two leaderboard identified CatBoost as the champion model with an AUC of zero point eight three zero six and a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. The optimised hyperparameters were depth six, six hundred fifty-seven iterations, L-two leaf regularisation of three point five one, and a learning rate of zero point zero zero six one seven, using RobustScaler and ordinal detailed plus raw pathology encoding. The current paper takes this frozen model as its starting point and applies the explainability and clinical translation analyses without any further model modification.
article-b-methods-sub-4: The fourth methods subsection describes the multi-method explainability framework, which is the intellectual centrepiece of the paper. Three complementary explainable AI methods were applied to the frozen CatBoost classifier. First, TreeSHAP, building on the foundational work of Lundberg and Lee who introduced a unified approach to interpreting model predictions in twenty seventeen, was computed on the full test set to derive both global feature importance as mean absolute SHAP values and local explanations for individual patients. Second, Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations, developed by Ribeiro, Singh, and Guestrin in twenty sixteen, were generated by perturbing each test instance with a Gaussian neighbourhood, fitting a sparse linear surrogate, and extracting per-feature local weights. Third, one-way partial-dependence plots, following the framework described by Molnar in his Interpretable Machine Learning textbook and formalised in the R Journal by Greenwell, were generated for the top-ranking predictors using the standard marginal expectation framework. The authors additionally inspected accumulated-local-effects-style behaviour, following the work of Apley and Zhu, for features showing strong potential interactions. Cross-method concordance was quantified by comparing the rank ordering and sign of contributions for the top eight features across all three methods.
article-b-methods-sub-5: The fifth methods subsection covers calibration assessment and post-hoc recalibration. Probability calibration was assessed for the five top-performing models from the leaderboard: CatBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and the Stacking Ensemble. The seventy-thirty split was used, with one thousand six hundred eighty-nine training and seven hundred twenty-four test observations. For each model, reliability diagrams were constructed using ten equal-frequency bins of predicted probability, plotting the mean predicted value against the observed fraction of positive outcomes, following the calibration evaluation principles articulated by Van Calster and colleagues and Steyerberg and colleagues. The Brier score, originally proposed by Brier in nineteen fifty, was computed as the mean squared error between predicted probabilities and binary outcomes. As a sensitivity analysis, Platt scaling, following the method described by Platt in nineteen ninety-nine, and isotonic regression, following Niculescu-Mizil and Caruana from twenty-oh-five, were applied to evaluate whether post-hoc recalibration further reduced the Brier score and improved reliability. This comprehensive calibration approach addresses what Van Calster and colleagues have called the Achilles heel of predictive analytics: the gap between high discrimination and well-calibrated absolute probability estimates.
article-b-methods-sub-6: The sixth methods subsection describes the decision curve analysis framework. Following the seminal methodology of Vickers and Elkin from twenty-oh-six, the authors computed net benefit for two hundred evenly spaced threshold probabilities ranging from zero point zero one to zero point nine nine. The net benefit formula is: true positives divided by total sample size, minus false positives divided by total sample size, multiplied by the threshold probability divided by one minus the threshold probability. The two reference strategies were treat-all, meaning every patient undergoes micro-TESE without a model, and treat-none, meaning no patient undergoes the procedure. The authors further translated net benefit into implied net interventions saved per one hundred patients using the standard transformation. Critically, the clinically meaningful threshold range for preoperative counselling was defined a priori as zero point two zero to zero point four zero, reflecting reasonable variation in patient and clinician preferences for accepting an unsuccessful procedure. This a priori threshold definition is important because it prevents post-hoc cherry-picking of favourable threshold ranges and anchors the clinical utility analysis in real-world decision-making contexts where patients and clinicians weigh the costs of an unsuccessful surgical attempt against the potential benefit of retrieving viable spermatozoa.
article-b-methods-sub-7: The seventh methods subsection specifies the clinical decision support system. The system is intended as a stateless preoperative counselling aid, not as an automated decision-maker. The input feature set was restricted to eleven evidence-supported variables selected on the basis of multi-method explainable AI rank, biological plausibility, and routine availability in tertiary fertility clinics. These eleven features are: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, Luteinising Hormone, Testosterone, Estradiol, the Testosterone-to-Estradiol ratio which is auto-computed, Inhibin B, Testicular Volume Left, Testicular Volume Right, Age, Body Mass Index calculated from height and weight, and Karyotype or Klinefelter status. Each feature carries a reference range, a validation colour code using red for under-range, teal for in-range, and orange for over-range, and a specific scoring function. For example, FSH and LH use a lower-is-better scoring function, Testosterone and Inhibin B use a higher-is-better function, and Estradiol uses a centred scoring function. The target architecture is PHP eight point zero or above on cPanel shared hosting with no database, no authentication, and no data persistence, using Bootstrap five point three point three and Chart JS four point four point three for the front-end. This deliberately lightweight architecture maximises accessibility in resource-constrained settings.
article-b-methods-sub-8: The eighth methods subsection addresses cross-method concordance metrics. The authors explain their deliberate choice to characterise concordance qualitatively rather than through a single numerical index, because the three explainability methods, SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence plots, operate on fundamentally different scales and assumptions. A forced numerical concordance metric would be misleading. Instead, they ranked features by three criteria: mean absolute SHAP value globally, median absolute LIME weight across all test-set instances, and the magnitude of the partial-dependence-derived effect, measured as the maximum PDP value minus the minimum PDP value across the observed feature range. Agreement was then reported as the set intersection of the top-eight rank lists and as the consistency of the sign of the leading contribution. For each of the five local cases highlighted in the SHAP analysis, the authors additionally compared the SHAP and LIME top-four contributors and their signs. They note that cases where signs disagreed would have been flagged for further inspection, but no such disagreement was observed across any of the five representative cases. This methodological transparency about the limitations of concordance measurement is itself a strength of the paper.
article-b-methods-sub-9: The ninth and final methods subsection covers statistical reporting and software. All analyses were performed in Python three point eleven using scikit-learn one point four, LightGBM four point x, XGBoost two point x, CatBoost one point two, SHAP zero point four five, and LIME zero point two. Confidence intervals for the Area Under the Curve were computed by bootstrap resampling with one thousand bootstrap samples. Decision curves were generated using the dcurves package and independently reproduced from the underlying net-benefit formula. All random seeds were fixed at forty-two to ensure reproducibility. Reporting follows the TRIPOD plus AI checklist, as described by Collins, Moons, Dhiman, and colleagues in their twenty twenty-four British Medical Journal publication. Risk of bias was self-assessed using PROBAST, following Wolff, Moons, Riley, and colleagues, and the PROBAST plus AI extension by Moons, Damen, Kaul, and colleagues from twenty twenty-five. All figures were generated with matplotlib and seaborn at publication-quality six hundred dots per inch. No data augmentation techniques were used other than SMOTE for class-imbalance handling within training folds, and no external pretrained models or transfer learning were applied. This level of computational transparency supports the reproducibility standards expected by JMIR Medical Informatics.
article-b-results: The Results section is organised into four thematic blocks spanning twelve subsections: cohort characteristics and model performance baseline, multi-method explainability covering global SHAP, local SHAP and LIME, partial-dependence behaviour, and cross-method concordance, probability calibration including sensitivity to recalibration, and decision-curve clinical utility with implied interventions saved and the clinical decision support system feature mapping. The headline findings are that the CatBoost model achieved an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six, the top four predictors, Luteinising Hormone, Age, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, and Testosterone, were stable across all three explainability methods, Brier scores for the five top models ranged from zero point two zero one for LightGBM to zero point two two six for the Stacking Ensemble, and decision curve analysis showed positive net clinical benefit across threshold probabilities of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. Notably, local SHAP explanations revealed that the model adapts its reasoning to the dominant signal available in each individual patient, a behaviour invisible at the population level but crucial for per-patient clinical counselling.
article-b-results-sub-1: The first results subsection reports cohort characteristics and the model performance baseline. Of two thousand four hundred fifty screened records, two thousand four hundred thirteen were retained after excluding thirty-seven records with missing Outcome One. The success rate of sperm retrieval at first micro-TESE was thirty-six point seven percent, corresponding to eight hundred eighty-six successes out of two thousand four hundred thirteen. On the version two leaderboard, CatBoost achieved the best discrimination with an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six, a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five, accuracy of zero point seven seven, sensitivity of zero point six seven eight, specificity of zero point eight two three, F-one score of zero point six eight four, and a Matthews correlation coefficient of zero point five zero. Following CatBoost, XGBoost achieved an AUC of zero point eight two one four, LightGBM reached zero point eight two one three, Random Forest achieved zero point eight one five seven, and Logistic Regression scored zero point seven nine two six. The Youden J optimal decision threshold for CatBoost lies in the range zero point four four four to zero point five zero seven. These baseline metrics establish the discriminative foundation upon which the subsequent explainability, calibration, and clinical utility analyses are built.
article-b-results-sub-2: The second results subsection presents the global SHAP feature importance analysis, which is summarised in Table Two and visualised in Figure Two. The top four mean absolute SHAP values were Luteinising Hormone at zero point three one seven, Age at zero point two seven zero, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone at zero point two five zero, and Testosterone at zero point two one two. There is a clear gap to the next tier: Body Weight at zero point one seven four, Sakamoto left testicular volume at zero point one seven one, Body Mass Index at zero point one six nine, and Height at zero point one six six. Sonographic and clinical testicular volume measures contributed an intermediate amount, with right testicular ultrasound volume at zero point one five six, clinical left volume at zero point one two nine, clinical right volume at zero point one one one, and left ultrasound volume at zero point zero eight five. Estradiol at zero point zero four three, Sakamoto right volume at zero point zero three nine, and Prolactin at zero point zero two zero made small but non-zero contributions. Crucially, the remaining thirteen candidate variables, including pathology, race, comorbidity flags, and lifestyle variables, had mean absolute SHAP values of essentially zero. This reflects both the right-skewed prevalence of these flags and the deliberate exclusion of intra-operative pathology from the preoperative model.
article-b-results-sub-3: The third results subsection examines local SHAP and LIME explanations for individual patients, providing the per-patient reasoning that population-level summaries cannot capture. Five representative test-set patients were analysed. In case two one six two, with a predicted probability of zero point seven eight two two and actual successful retrieval, the model leveraged elevated Age at fifty-six years contributing plus zero point five six seven, elevated FSH at ten point eight contributing plus zero point four one one, and elevated LH at nine point one seven contributing plus zero point one eight one, balanced against low Testosterone at two point one contributing minus zero point zero eight six. In case seven six five, with a predicted probability of zero point two eight three six and actual failure, the dominant contributor was Age at forty years contributing minus zero point three two zero. In the remarkable case one eight three three, with a predicted probability of zero point eight eight three six, the largest local driver was right testicular ultrasound volume at two thousand two hundred twenty-three cubic millimetres contributing plus two point zero six zero, demonstrating that the model can exploit ultrasound information when hormonal signals are equivocal. LIME recapitulated these patterns, with the top four contributors and their signs agreeing with SHAP in all five cases. LIME magnitudes were smaller, as expected from its sparse linear-surrogate construction.
article-b-results-sub-4: The fourth results subsection describes the partial-dependence behaviour for the top predictors, visualised in Figure Three. For Luteinising Hormone and Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, the partial-dependence plots showed monotonically increasing risk-of-failure SHAP values above clinical reference thresholds of approximately nine milliinternational units per millilitre for LH and twelve to fifteen for FSH. This monotonic pattern is consistent with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, the canonical endocrine signature of severe spermatogenic failure. Testosterone showed a monotonically decreasing risk pattern up to mid-range values, plateauing above approximately four nanograms per millilitre. The Age partial-dependence plot was distinctly non-monotone, with the most favourable region between roughly thirty and forty years, and worsening contributions at both extremes. This aligns with the clinical observation that very young non-obstructive azoospermia patients often carry severe primary spermatogenic phenotypes while very old patients experience additional age-related testicular decline. Body Weight and Body Mass Index showed mildly U-shaped patterns centred near the normal range. Bilateral testicular-volume plots were monotonically favourable with increasing volume but plateaued above approximately twelve millilitres per testicle, consistent with the andrological literature cited by Wang and colleagues.
article-b-results-sub-5: The fifth results subsection addresses cross-method concordance of feature importance. When the authors compared the SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence-derived rankings, the top four predictors, Luteinising Hormone, Age, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, and Testosterone, were identical across all three methods, as documented in Table Three. Beyond the top four, anthropometric and ultrasound-volume measures clustered together in ranks five through nine across all methods, with only minor reshuffling. An important finding was that Seminal plasma pH, ranked eleventh globally by SHAP with a value of zero point one two eight, was frequently elevated to the top five in LIME explanations for specific patients, illustrating that global averages can mask important local effects. Estradiol contributed modestly in all three methods but emerged more visibly in LIME explanations of cases with abnormal Testosterone-to-Estradiol balance. The overall conclusion is that the multi-method approach yields a method-stable and biologically interpretable set of preoperative drivers for micro-TESE outcome. This concordance is not trivial to achieve because each method operates under different assumptions: SHAP guarantees additivity through Shapley values, LIME fits sparse local surrogates, and partial-dependence plots compute marginal expectations. Agreement across all three provides genuine robustness evidence.
article-b-results-sub-6: The sixth results subsection reports probability calibration for the five top-performing models. Brier scores were zero point two zero one for LightGBM, zero point two zero two for Gradient Boosting, zero point two zero five for XGBoost, zero point two one three for CatBoost, and zero point two two six for the Stacking Ensemble, as detailed in Table Five and visualised in Figure Five. LightGBM and Gradient Boosting tracked the diagonal reliability line most closely across the full range of predicted probabilities. CatBoost was well calibrated except for a mild downward deflection in the highest decile, indicating slight over-confidence at the high end, a recurring pattern for gradient-boosted classifiers. The Stacking Ensemble showed clearer over-confidence with the observed positive fraction collapsing in the top bin. All five Brier scores remained well below the non-informative reference of zero point three six seven, which corresponds to a class-prevalence-only predictor at a thirty-six point seven percent positive rate. This demonstrates that all five models carry meaningful probabilistic information. The calibration analysis addresses what Van Calster and colleagues have termed the most under-reported dimension of clinical machine learning evaluation, despite its critical importance for shared decision-making.
article-b-results-sub-7: The seventh results subsection presents the decision curve analysis findings. The CatBoost champion model demonstrated positive net clinical benefit across threshold probabilities of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. At a threshold of zero point zero five, CatBoost net benefit was zero point three two six while the treat-all reference was zero point three three zero. At zero point one zero, CatBoost achieved zero point two eight five against treat-all at zero point two seven zero. At zero point two zero, CatBoost reached zero point two zero eight while treat-all dropped to zero point one five nine. At the clinically important threshold of zero point three zero, CatBoost maintained a net benefit of zero point one four four while treat-all had fallen to just zero point zero four three. At zero point four zero, CatBoost net benefit was zero point zero eight four while treat-all was already negative at minus zero point one zero seven. LightGBM achieved the highest net benefit in the lower-to-mid range, reaching zero point three three four at threshold zero point zero five and zero point one six one at zero point three zero. The Stacking Ensemble underperformed in the mid-range, with only zero point one zero one at threshold zero point three zero, consistent with its inferior calibration. The decision curve analysis confirmed that all five models offered substantial clinical utility within the preoperative counselling window of zero point two zero to zero point four zero.
article-b-results-sub-8: The eighth results subsection translates the decision curve analysis into a more clinically tangible metric: implied unnecessary interventions saved per one hundred patients. Using the standard transformation, the authors calculated how many fewer unnecessary surgeries would be performed using the model compared with a treat-all strategy. At a threshold of zero point two zero, CatBoost implied approximately four point nine fewer unnecessary surgeries per one hundred patients, while LightGBM implied approximately six point seven fewer. At a threshold of zero point three zero, the implied savings increased to approximately ten point one for CatBoost and eleven point eight for LightGBM per one hundred patients. At a threshold of zero point four zero, the savings reached approximately nineteen point one for CatBoost and twenty-one point three for LightGBM per one hundred patients. The authors appropriately caution that these numbers represent net effects at the chosen threshold and depend on patient-specific preferences. Nonetheless, they convey the magnitude of clinical resource and patient-burden benefit that a transparent model-aided counselling protocol can deliver compared with a default operate-always strategy. For a fertility clinic performing hundreds of micro-TESE procedures annually, these intervention savings translate directly into reduced surgical complications, anaesthetic exposure, and psychological distress for patients with low probability of success.
article-b-results-sub-9: The ninth results subsection examines sensitivity to post-hoc recalibration. Platt scaling produced a noticeable improvement only for the Stacking Ensemble model, reducing its Brier score from zero point two two six to approximately zero point two one four and yielding a more nearly monotone reliability curve. For the tree-based ensembles, CatBoost, LightGBM, XGBoost, and Gradient Boosting, Platt scaling either preserved the Brier score within plus or minus zero point zero zero five or marginally improved it without changing the qualitative reliability pattern. Isotonic regression produced visually smoother calibration curves but introduced step artefacts at low predicted-probability ranges in two of the five models, suggesting overfitting to the calibration set. Based on these findings, the authors prefer Platt scaling for production deployment, with a recommendation to re-fit it on prospectively collected local data every twelve months. This pragmatic recommendation reflects the reality that a model deployed at a centre with a different baseline success rate will drift in calibration, and periodic recalibration is a low-cost but high-impact maintenance step.
article-b-results-sub-10: The tenth results subsection reports on the robustness of the explainable AI evidence to model resampling. Although the paper takes the version two CatBoost model as a frozen artefact, the authors additionally inspected the stability of the SHAP rankings against bootstrap resampling of the test set using two hundred bootstrap replicates. The top four global ranks, Luteinising Hormone, Age, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone, and Testosterone, were preserved in one hundred percent of bootstrap replicates. Ranks five through eight, comprising Body Weight, Sakamoto left testicular volume, Body Mass Index, and Height, interchanged occasionally but never moved outside the top-eight set. The bilateral testicular-volume measures occupied ranks nine through thirteen with stable membership. Features with mean absolute SHAP below zero point zero five, specifically Estradiol, Sakamoto right volume, and Prolactin, showed greater rank instability but their contributions remained uniformly small. This bootstrap robustness check is an important methodological contribution because it demonstrates that the multi-method explainability narrative is not artefactual to a particular test-set sampling. The one hundred percent preservation rate for the top four features across two hundred replicates provides strong evidence of a stable and genuine importance hierarchy.
article-b-results-sub-11: The eleventh results subsection explores the heterogeneity of local explanations across the predicted-probability spectrum. Examining local SHAP explanations for five representative cases revealed that the model does not uniformly weight features across patients but adapts its reasoning to the dominant signal available in each case. At low predicted probability, case seven six five with a predicted value of zero point two eight three six, the dominant contribution was demographic, specifically Age. At intermediate probability, case two three eight seven with predicted value zero point four two nine nine, the model used Seminal plasma pH as a strong negative contributor with a SHAP value of minus zero point seven two one zero, counteracting a positive Age signal. At moderate-to-high probability, case two one six two with predicted value zero point seven eight two two, the model combined Age, FSH, and LH as positive contributors in the canonical hypergonadotropic pattern. At high probability, case one eight three three with predicted value zero point eight eight three six, the model relied on sonographic volume measurements rather than hormones. This adaptive reasoning is clinically significant because it means the model provides genuinely personalised explanations rather than applying a fixed recipe. It is precisely this behaviour that per-patient SHAP analysis can expose and that population-level metrics obscure.
article-b-results-sub-12: The twelfth and final results subsection describes the clinical decision support system feature mapping and integrated workflow. Combining the explainable AI evidence, calibration analysis, and decision-curve-derived clinically useful threshold window, the authors operationalised an eleven-feature clinical decision support system detailed in Table Four. Hormonal inputs carry the largest weight, consistent with the SHAP ranking: FSH with a reference range of one point five to twelve point four milliinternational units per millilitre, LH with one point seven to eight point six, Testosterone with three hundred to one thousand nanograms per decilitre, Estradiol with ten to forty picograms per millilitre, and the auto-computed Testosterone-to-Estradiol ratio. Inhibin B, with a reference range of eighty to three hundred picograms per millilitre, is included with a hardcoded weight of zero point one two. Bilateral testicular volume is retained as complementary anatomical signal. Age, Body Mass Index, and Karyotype provide demographic and genetic context. Partner Age was excluded because its high SHAP contribution in early iterations reflected confounding with treatment urgency, not a causal biological mechanism. The patient-facing output combines a calibrated probability gauge with colour bands tied to the decision-curve-derived threshold range of zero point two zero to zero point four zero, a bar chart of the top four SHAP contributors, and structured counselling text.
article-b-discussion: The Discussion synthesises the study's contributions and situates them within the broader literature. Three findings are emphasised. First, the top four predictors, LH, Age, FSH, and Testosterone, were identical and stable across SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence analyses, providing a method-independent biological signal. The authors note this is biologically coherent: elevated LH and FSH with subnormal testosterone form the canonical endocrine signature of hypergonadotropic hypogonadism in non-obstructive azoospermia, consistent with the testosterone-to-LH ratio literature by Kim and Koo and the testosterone-to-FSH ratio work by Siva and colleagues. Second, calibration was acceptable for tree-based models with Brier scores of zero point two zero one to zero point two one three, while only the Stacking Ensemble required Platt scaling. Third, decision curve analysis demonstrated meaningful net benefit in the counselling window zero point two zero to zero point four zero. The Discussion also addresses the critical exclusion of Partner Age, which had a deceptively large SHAP importance of zero point three zero one in earlier iterations but lacks any biological mechanism affecting testicular spermatogenesis. The authors invoke the broader systematic review by Tun and colleagues on trust in AI-based clinical decision support systems, identifying transparent explanation, demonstrated calibration, and clinical utility as the three preconditions for adoption, all directly addressed by this paper. Limitations include single-centre design and the need for prospective external validation.
article-b-conclusions: The Conclusions section delivers three connected messages. First, the multi-method explainability analysis of the CatBoost model, with an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six and a Brier score of zero point two one three, revealed a method-stable and biologically coherent predictor set dominated by Luteinising Hormone at a mean absolute SHAP of zero point three one seven, Age at zero point two seven zero, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone at zero point two five zero, and Testosterone at zero point two one two. Probability calibration was acceptable for all top-five models with Brier scores ranging from zero point two zero one to zero point two two six, and decision curve analysis demonstrated positive net benefit across threshold probabilities of zero point zero five to zero point six zero. Second, these findings are operationalised in an eleven-feature clinical decision support framework with explicit reference ranges, colour-coded input validation, transparent scoring functions, and a stateless web-deliverable architecture designed for resource-constrained fertility clinics. Third, and most broadly, the paper illustrates a pragmatic, reproducible template for translating opaque machine learning classifiers into clinically credible decision-support tools in reproductive medicine. The authors conclude that as prediction models proliferate in clinical practice, the principal challenge is no longer demonstrating discrimination but demonstrating that the model can be trusted, understood, and acted upon.

article-b-probast: The PROBAST assessment, following the framework by Wolff, Moons, Riley, and colleagues with the AI extension by Moons, Damen, Kaul, and colleagues from twenty twenty-five, evaluates risk of bias across seven domains. The Participants domain is rated low risk: the single-centre Royan Institute retrospective cohort has explicit inclusion of confirmed non-obstructive azoospermia patients with documented exclusion criteria and two thousand four hundred thirteen retained for analysis. The Predictors domain is rated low risk: all twenty-nine candidates were collected as routine pre-micro-TESE workup before outcome was known, and pathology variables that could leak postoperative information were either downweighted to near-zero SHAP or re-introduced through structured preoperative encoding in the clinical decision support system. The Outcome domain is rated low risk: Outcome One is clinician-confirmed binary retrieval captured from operative records, assessed by staff blinded to model predictions. The Analysis domain is rated low risk: eight hundred eighty-six events provide ample statistical support, k-nearest-neighbours imputation with missingness indicators is applied inside cross-validation folds, and SMOTE is training-fold-only. The AI algorithm transparency domain is rated low risk with fully specified hyperparameters and fixed random seed. The AI data quality domain is also rated low risk. Critically, the AI robustness and generalisability domain is rated high risk because no external multicentre validation has been performed. The overall assessment is low risk of bias for development but high concern for generalisability pending external validation.
home-hero: Welcome to the NOA Predictive Analytics Dashboard, a comprehensive visualization of an entire machine learning research pipeline dedicated to one of the most challenging problems in reproductive medicine. What you are about to explore represents the intersection of advanced artificial intelligence and clinical urology, specifically the prediction of microsurgical testicular sperm extraction outcomes in men diagnosed with non-obstructive azoospermia. Let us set the clinical stage. Non-obstructive azoospermia, often abbreviated NOA, affects approximately one percent of all men and accounts for roughly fifteen percent of male infertility cases. These are patients whose ejaculate contains no spermatozoa, not because of a physical obstruction in the reproductive tract, but because of a fundamental failure in the biological process of spermatogenesis itself. For these men, the gold-standard surgical intervention is micro-TESE, a meticulous procedure where a reproductive urologist operates under high-powered magnification to identify and extract individual seminiferous tubules that may still harbor viable sperm. The challenge, however, is that micro-TESE success rates are notoriously variable, typically ranging from thirty to sixty percent across different centers and patient populations. This variability creates a profound clinical dilemma. Every unsuccessful micro-TESE means a patient has undergone an invasive surgical procedure with its associated risks, costs, and emotional toll, only to learn that no sperm could be recovered. Consider for a moment the psychological weight carried by these couples, many of whom have already endured years of infertility diagnosis and treatment. What this dashboard presents is a rigorous attempt to address that uncertainty through machine learning. Using data from two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, one of the most prestigious fertility centers in the world, the research team developed and validated predictive models that can estimate a patient's probability of successful sperm retrieval before they enter the operating room. The pipeline you will explore encompasses everything from raw data preprocessing and novel natural language processing of pathology reports, through systematic evaluation of sixteen machine learning algorithms, to a clinically deployable decision support system. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the PhD-level ambition behind it. This is not a simple proof-of-concept study. It represents a complete research arc from clinical problem identification through methodological innovation to translational application, all presented through this interactive dashboard that itself serves as a demonstration of technical sophistication. The research bridges reproductive medicine and artificial intelligence in a way that contributes meaningfully to both fields.
home-stat-cohort: Two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients. This number serves as the bedrock upon which everything you will explore in this dashboard is built. Every model, every validation curve, every SHAP analysis, every clinical recommendation traces back to the clinical records of these two thousand four hundred and thirteen individuals who underwent micro-TESE at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine. Now, a natural question arises: is two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients sufficient for a machine learning study? The answer, like most things in clinical research, requires nuance. In the realm of rare-condition clinical machine learning, sample sizes are fundamentally constrained by disease prevalence and the specificity of the intervention being studied. Non-obstructive azoospermia, while affecting roughly one percent of all men, represents a relatively uncommon diagnosis in absolute terms, and the subset of those patients who proceed to micro-TESE at any single institution over a given period is smaller still. What makes this cohort particularly valuable is its single-center origin. Every patient was evaluated, operated on, and followed up at the same institution, by clinicians adhering to the same surgical protocols, the same pathology reporting standards, and the same clinical decision-making frameworks. This consistency eliminates a substantial source of noise that plagues multi-center studies, where differences in surgical technique, tissue handling, and pathological interpretation can introduce confounding variation that obscures the signal machine learning algorithms are trying to detect. Notice how this design choice involves a deliberate trade-off. The consistency gained from single-center data comes at the cost of generalizability. A model trained exclusively on Royan Institute patients may not perform identically when applied to patients from institutions with different surgical approaches or patient demographics. The research acknowledges this limitation transparently, which is itself a mark of methodological integrity. Consider also that these two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients represent a carefully curated analytical cohort, not the raw database. The original records were processed through rigorous quality checks, missingness evaluation, and outcome verification before a patient was included. This curation process ensures that every case in the dataset has complete, verified outcome data, which is essential for training reliable prediction models. For rare-condition clinical machine learning, two thousand four hundred and thirteen carefully curated, consistently collected cases from a world-renowned center represent a meaningful and defensible foundation for model development and validation.
home-stat-features: Fifty-eight features. Think of this number as representing the comprehensive clinical fingerprint of each patient in this study. Every one of those two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients is characterized not by a handful of obvious variables, but by a rich, multidimensional portrait that captures the complexity of their clinical presentation. These fifty-eight features span multiple clinical domains, and understanding their composition reveals the depth of the data engineering effort behind this research. You have demographic variables like patient age. You have anthropometric measurements including height, body weight, and body mass index. The hormonal profile is particularly rich, encompassing follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, testosterone, estradiol, and prolactin, each of which provides a window into the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis that governs spermatogenesis. Testicular measurements capture the physical characteristics of the testes through multiple modalities: ultrasound dimensions, orchidometer readings, and calculated Sakamoto volumes. Surgical history documents prior interventions, varicocele status, and other urological procedures. Genetic factors including karyotype analysis and Y chromosome microdeletion subtypes add another layer of biological information. But what makes this feature set truly distinctive is the inclusion of eighteen pathology features extracted through a custom natural language processing pipeline. Raw free-text pathology reports, the kind dictated by pathologists in natural clinical language, were systematically parsed into structured, quantitative variables capturing specific histological patterns: Sertoli cell only syndrome, complete seminiferous tubular hyalinization, maturation arrest at four distinct developmental stages, and hypospermatogenesis, each recorded separately for the right and left testis. Consider for a moment the intellectual work embedded in this transformation. Raw clinical records, messy and unstructured by nature, were carefully engineered into this structured feature set through a combination of domain expertise, regex-based natural language processing, and hierarchical encoding logic. The result is a feature space that captures both the conventional clinical variables used in standard practice and the novel pathology-derived indicators that previous studies either ignored entirely or reduced to a single categorical label. Each of these fifty-eight features earns its place through a combination of clinical relevance and statistical contribution to the prediction task. The journey from fifty-five raw columns to fifty-eight engineered features is itself a story of careful, deliberate clinical data science.
home-stat-best-model: CatBoost version two. This is the headline result, the champion model that emerged from a rigorous tournament of sixteen machine learning algorithms, each optimized through Bayesian hyperparameter tuning and validated through five-by-five nested cross-validation. When you see CatBoost v2 displayed here, you are looking at the culmination of the entire research pipeline, the model that best learned to predict micro-TESE outcomes from the clinical data. CatBoost, which stands for Categorical Boosting, is a gradient boosting framework developed by Yandex that brings several architectural innovations particularly well-suited to clinical tabular data. Unlike its cousins XGBoost and LightGBM, CatBoost implements ordered boosting, a technique that reduces prediction shift and overfitting by using a permutation-driven approach during training. It also handles categorical features natively through target-based encoding with randomized permutations, avoiding the information leakage that can occur with naive target encoding. What makes this result particularly noteworthy is the journey that led here. The version two designation is significant. An earlier pipeline, version one, had identified LightGBM as the top performer with an AUC of zero point seven three two seven. However, a critical data leakage bug in the preprocessing, specifically an IQR clipping error that zeroed out pathology features, rendered those results invalid. The version two pipeline corrected this issue, incorporated the full eighteen pathology features through proper ordinal encoding, and re-evaluated all sixteen models. CatBoost emerged as the clear winner with an AUC of zero point eight three zero six, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval spanning zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. Notice how this narrative itself teaches an important lesson about computational research. The willingness to identify, acknowledge, and correct a critical pipeline error, even when it means discarding previously reported results, demonstrates the kind of scientific integrity that distinguishes rigorous research from results-driven model fitting. The v2 CatBoost model is not just a better number; it is a better, more trustworthy result built on a corrected and validated pipeline.
home-stat-figures: This dashboard contains a comprehensive collection of figures, charts, and interactive visualizations, and each one serves a deliberate purpose in telling the complete research story. In clinical machine learning, visualization is not merely decorative. It is epistemic. It transforms abstract numerical results into interpretable, scrutinizable evidence that clinicians, reviewers, and fellow researchers can engage with critically. Consider the range of visual analytics represented here. You will encounter data exploration plots that reveal the distributional characteristics of the cohort, from hormone level distributions to pathology pattern frequencies across the two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients. Model comparison charts present the sixteen-algorithm tournament results in formats that make performance differences immediately apparent. ROC curves and precision-recall curves illustrate the trade-offs each model makes between sensitivity and specificity. Calibration plots assess whether predicted probabilities align with observed frequencies, a critical but often overlooked aspect of clinical prediction models. Decision curve analyses translate statistical performance into clinical utility by quantifying net benefit across different decision thresholds. The explainability visualizations deserve special attention. SHAP summary plots, dependence plots, and waterfall charts make the internal logic of the CatBoost model transparent, showing exactly which features drive predictions and how. These are not post-hoc rationalizations but mathematically grounded decompositions of the model's decision process using TreeSHAP, the algorithm specifically designed for tree-based ensemble models. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the philosophy underlying the visualization approach. Every figure was designed to communicate a specific aspect of the research pipeline, from raw data characteristics through model selection to clinical translation. Together, they create a visual narrative that complements the statistical findings, making complex algorithms interpretable for clinicians who may not have deep machine learning expertise but who need to understand and trust the predictions before applying them to patient care. This transparency is not optional in clinical AI; it is a prerequisite for responsible deployment. Each visualization in this gallery was crafted to serve both the rigor demanded by peer review and the clarity demanded by clinical audiences.
home-architecture-banner: What you are seeing here is not simply a static research presentation. This dashboard is powered by a multi-agent artificial intelligence architecture, a coordinated system of specialized AI agents working in concert to synthesize, analyze, and present the research findings you will explore. This architectural approach represents a novel contribution in its own right, extending beyond the clinical machine learning research to demonstrate how AI agent swarms can be leveraged for complex knowledge presentation tasks. The concept is analogous to an expert panel discussion. Rather than relying on a single monolithic system to handle everything from literature review to statistical analysis to narrative generation, the architecture decomposes these responsibilities into specialized agents, each optimized for a specific domain. A review agent handles literature synthesis, identifying relevant prior studies, extracting methodological details, and situating the present research within the broader academic landscape. A statistics agent performs quantitative analysis, generating metrics, validation results, and comparative evaluations with mathematical precision. An orchestrator agent coordinates the entire ensemble, managing data flow between agents, resolving conflicts in interpretation, and ensuring that the final output is coherent and internally consistent. Consider for a moment what this means for the PhD defense context. The dashboard itself becomes a deliverable, a demonstration of technical capability in artificial intelligence that complements the clinical machine learning research being presented. The defense committee encounters not just the results of the micro-TESE prediction study, but a sophisticated AI system that synthesizes and presents those results, showcasing expertise in both clinical data science and modern AI systems architecture. This multi-agent approach also addresses a practical challenge in research presentation: maintaining consistency across dozens of visualizations, narratives, and analytical outputs. The orchestrator ensures that every number cited, every confidence interval quoted, and every clinical interpretation offered is drawn from the same validated source, the project knowledge base, eliminating the inconsistencies that can creep into large, manually assembled presentations. This shared source of truth is what guarantees that the AUC of zero point eight three zero six you see in one section matches precisely what appears in every other section throughout the dashboard.
home-phase-cards: These phase cards lay out the complete research pipeline, and walking through them reveals the structured, systematic progression from raw clinical data to a deployable clinical decision support system. Each card represents not just a methodological step but months of careful, iterative work. The first phase encompasses data collection and preprocessing. This is where two thousand four hundred and fifty raw patient records from Royan Institute's electronic medical records were ingested, cleaned, and refined into an analytical cohort of two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients with complete outcome data. Nine columns with one hundred percent missingness were dropped. Remaining missing values were handled through k-nearest neighbors imputation fitted exclusively within cross-validation folds to prevent data leakage. The second phase, feature engineering, is where much of the intellectual novelty resides. A custom natural language processing pipeline parsed free-text pathology reports into eighteen structured features, capturing bilateral histological patterns with a hierarchical maturation arrest encoding logic that respects the biological progression of spermatogenesis. This produced the fifty-eight feature set that characterizes each patient. The third phase involved model training and selection across sixteen distinct algorithms spanning six algorithmic families, from simple logistic regression through gradient boosting frameworks to neural networks and meta-ensembles. Each model underwent Bayesian hyperparameter optimization within the inner loop of a five-by-five nested cross-validation design. The fourth phase, rigorous validation, subjected the top-performing models to bootstrap validation, calibration analysis, decision curve analysis, and learning curve assessment, ten of eleven planned validation tests completed successfully. The fifth phase brought explainability through SHAP and TreeSHAP analysis, decomposing the CatBoost model's predictions into feature-level contributions that clinicians can understand and scrutinize. Notice how the final phase, clinical translation, connects everything to a practical endpoint: the clinical decision support system. The CDSS takes the validated CatBoost model and wraps it in an interface that allows clinicians to input patient features and receive predicted probabilities of successful sperm retrieval. Each phase feeds directly into the next, creating a coherent pipeline where every decision at one stage has implications for subsequent stages.
about-team: The research behind this dashboard exemplifies the interdisciplinary collaboration that modern medical artificial intelligence demands. No single discipline holds all the expertise required to take a clinical prediction problem from raw patient records to a validated, explainable, and deployable decision support tool. This work required, and received, contributions spanning reproductive urology, clinical pathology, biostatistics, machine learning engineering, and software development. At the clinical foundation, the work depends entirely on the expertise of reproductive urologists and andrologists at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine. These are the clinicians who performed every micro-TESE procedure in the dataset, who made the surgical decisions, and whose clinical judgment established the ground truth outcomes that the machine learning models learn to predict. Their domain expertise was equally critical during feature selection, where clinical knowledge determined which variables were biologically plausible predictors and which, like partner age, were potential confounders that should be excluded despite showing statistical associations. The pathology expertise deserves particular recognition. The free-text pathology reports that were parsed into the eighteen NLP-derived features were originally dictated by pathologists interpreting testicular biopsy tissue under the microscope. Understanding the clinical significance of Sertoli cell only patterns versus maturation arrest at different stages versus hypospermatogenesis requires years of specialized training. On the computational side, the research demanded expertise in clinical data science, including proficiency with gradient boosting frameworks, nested cross-validation design, SHAP-based model explanation, and careful attention to data leakage prevention. The PhD mentorship structure typical of medical informatics programs provided the scaffolding for this interdisciplinary integration, with supervisors bridging the gap between clinical insight and computational methodology. What makes this collaboration particularly effective is that it was not merely parallel work in separate silos. The clinicians and computational researchers engaged in genuine dialogue, where clinical knowledge shaped modeling decisions and modeling results informed clinical interpretation. This bidirectional exchange is the hallmark of translational medical AI research done well.
about-project: This project addresses one of the most frustrating uncertainties in reproductive medicine: the inability to reliably predict whether a micro-TESE procedure will successfully retrieve sperm from a patient with non-obstructive azoospermia. The clinical problem is straightforward to articulate but fiendishly difficult to solve. A couple presents to a fertility center. The male partner has no sperm in his ejaculate, and diagnostic workup confirms the cause is testicular rather than obstructive. The urologist recommends micro-TESE as the best available option. But the question that haunts both doctor and patient is this: will it work? Current clinical practice relies on a combination of hormonal markers, testicular examination findings, and histopathological patterns to estimate prognosis, but these assessments are subjective, inconsistent across centers, and lack the quantitative precision that evidence-based medicine demands. The research question driving this project is whether machine learning can do better, whether an algorithm trained on comprehensive clinical data can provide more accurate, more consistent, and more granular predictions than clinical gestalt alone. The answer, based on the evidence assembled here, is a cautious but confident yes. The version two CatBoost model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of zero point eight three zero six, validated through five-by-five nested cross-validation with Bayesian hyperparameter optimization, and demonstrated clinical utility through decision curve analysis across the therapeutically relevant threshold range. This performance was achieved on a cohort of two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients from the Royan Institute, with a baseline success rate of thirty-six point seven percent. But this project contributes more than a single performance metric. The methodological innovations include a custom NLP pathology parser with hierarchical maturation arrest staging, a systematic comparison of sixteen algorithms that sets a new standard for thoroughness in the field, TRIPOD and PROBAST compliant reporting that ensures transparency, and a clinical decision support system that translates the research into a tool clinicians can actually use. The IRB-approved study, conducted under grant identification number four zero two zero eight zero two, adheres to the Declaration of Helsinki and follows STROBE guidelines for observational research. What you are exploring in this dashboard is not just a model. It is a complete research arc from problem to solution, presented with the transparency and rigor that clinical AI demands.
arch-overview: This page reveals the system architecture powering the dashboard you are exploring, and it represents a genuinely novel approach to research presentation and synthesis. Rather than building a conventional static presentation, this project employs an AI agent swarm, a coordinated ensemble of specialized artificial intelligence agents, each responsible for a specific domain of the research pipeline. The concept of agent-based architecture has gained tremendous momentum in the AI community, and for good reason. Complex tasks that require expertise across multiple domains benefit from decomposition into specialized subtasks, each handled by an agent optimized for that particular type of reasoning. In this system, the specialization follows the natural divisions of academic research: literature review, statistical analysis, visualization, and narrative synthesis. What makes this architectural choice particularly interesting is the contrast with monolithic alternatives. A single large language model prompted to handle everything from citation formatting to statistical interpretation to clinical explanation would inevitably compromise on quality in at least some of those domains. By distributing responsibilities across purpose-built agents, the system achieves higher quality outputs in each domain while maintaining overall coherence through centralized orchestration. Notice how this architecture also addresses one of the most persistent challenges in AI-assisted research: factual consistency. When a single agent generates all content, numerical inconsistencies can creep in across different sections. The agent swarm architecture mitigates this by maintaining a shared knowledge base, the project brain, from which all agents draw their authoritative data. Every AUC value, every cohort size, every confidence interval cited anywhere in this dashboard originates from that single source of truth. Consider for a moment what this means for the future of academic research presentation. The dashboard you are exploring is itself a prototype for how complex, data-rich research projects might be communicated in the future: not through static slides or linear manuscripts alone, but through interactive, AI-orchestrated presentations that can adapt their depth and focus to the audience's needs.
arch-orchestrator: The orchestrator agent is the conductor of this AI ensemble, and understanding its role illuminates the principles of effective multi-agent system design. Just as a symphony orchestra requires a conductor to ensure that strings, brass, woodwinds, and percussion contribute their parts in harmony, this multi-agent architecture requires a central coordinating intelligence that manages task allocation, data flow, conflict resolution, and output integration. The orchestrator's primary responsibilities are threefold. First, it decomposes complex tasks into subtasks appropriate for each specialized agent. When the system needs to generate a comprehensive validation analysis, for example, the orchestrator identifies which components require statistical computation, which require literature comparison, and which require narrative explanation, then dispatches each component to the appropriate agent. Second, it manages data flow, ensuring that each agent receives the inputs it needs and that outputs from upstream agents are properly formatted and validated before being passed to downstream consumers. Third, and perhaps most critically, it performs conflict resolution. When different agents produce contradictory or inconsistent outputs, perhaps because they accessed different versions of a dataset or applied different interpretive frameworks, the orchestrator adjudicates these conflicts by reference to the authoritative project knowledge base. The parallels to distributed computing architectures are illuminating. The orchestrator functions similarly to a message broker in a microservices architecture, or a scheduler in a distributed computing framework. It maintains awareness of system state, tracks task completion, handles error recovery, and ensures that the final assembled output represents a coherent whole rather than a disconnected collection of individually generated fragments. What makes this orchestrator particularly sophisticated is its awareness of the academic context. It understands that the output must satisfy the exacting standards of a PhD defense committee, which means every claim must be traceable to evidence, every number must be consistent across all dashboard sections, and every interpretation must be defensible under expert scrutiny. This domain awareness distinguishes it from a generic task scheduler and positions it as a specialized research orchestration engine.
arch-agent-cards: Let us walk through the specialized agents that comprise this AI swarm, because each one brings distinct capabilities to the research synthesis task. Understanding their individual roles reveals the thought that went into decomposing a complex academic presentation into manageable, specialized subtasks. The review agent handles literature synthesis with the depth and critical perspective expected of doctoral-level scholarship. Its responsibilities include identifying relevant prior studies in micro-TESE outcome prediction, extracting methodological details for comparative analysis, assessing the strengths and limitations of existing approaches, and situating the present research within the broader landscape. When you encounter discussions of how this study's AUC of zero point eight three zero six compares to published benchmarks, or how the sixteen-model comparison exceeds the typical two-or-three model approach in the literature, that synthesis flows from the review agent's domain. The statistics agent operates with mathematical precision, handling quantitative analysis and metric generation. This agent computes and validates performance metrics, generates confidence intervals through bootstrap procedures, performs calibration assessments, and ensures that every statistical claim in the dashboard is numerically accurate. It understands the difference between apparent and optimism-corrected performance, between point estimates and interval estimates, and between statistical significance and clinical significance. The visualization agent transforms data and analysis results into the charts, plots, and interactive elements throughout this dashboard. It makes design decisions about color scales, axis ranges, annotation placement, and chart types that best communicate each specific finding. Notice how the output of each agent feeds into the work of others. The review agent's literature findings inform the statistics agent's comparative analyses. The statistics agent's metrics feed the visualization agent's charts. And the orchestrator ensures these handoffs occur smoothly, with data integrity maintained at each transition. This interconnected pipeline of intelligence creates outputs that no single agent could produce alone, each bringing specialized reasoning to its portion of the task while the collective produces a coherent, comprehensive research presentation.
articles-article-a: This paper by Jamalirad, Sabbaghian, and Vakili Arki presents what is, to the authors' knowledge, the largest pure machine learning study for predicting sperm retrieval success in microdissection testicular sperm extraction, commonly known as micro-TESE. The study draws on two thousand four hundred and thirteen consecutive patients with non-obstructive azoospermia treated at the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran, between two thousand seven and twenty twenty-two. The authors constructed a seventy-three-feature analytic matrix by augmenting standard clinical variables with eighteen newly engineered features derived from a custom natural language processing pipeline applied to free-text bilateral pathology reports. They benchmarked sixteen machine learning algorithms under a leakage-controlled five-by-five nested cross-validation design. The champion model, CatBoost, achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of zero point eight three zero six, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. The study is notable for its transparent disclosure of a version one pipeline data-leakage incident and its subsequent remediation, its adherence to TRIPOD plus AI twenty twenty-four and PROBAST plus AI twenty twenty-five reporting standards, and its SHAP-based interpretability analysis identifying luteinizing hormone, age, follicle-stimulating hormone, and testosterone as the dominant predictors. The paper targets Computers in Biology and Medicine and represents a significant methodological advance in reproductive medicine prediction modeling.
articles-article-b: This paper presents a multi-method explainable artificial intelligence framework for predicting sperm retrieval outcomes in microdissection testicular sperm extraction, commonly known as micro-TESE, integrated with a clinical decision support system designed for bedside use. The study, authored by Jamalirad, Sabbaghian, and Vakili Arki, analysed two thousand four hundred thirteen patients with non-obstructive azoospermia from the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran. The central model is a CatBoost classifier achieving an Area Under the Curve of zero point eight three zero six, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. What distinguishes this work from prior micro-TESE prediction studies is not a marginal discrimination gain but rather its systematic approach to clinical translation. The authors apply three complementary explainability methods, SHapley Additive exPlanations or SHAP, Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations or LIME, and partial-dependence plots, demonstrating concordant feature rankings across all three. They further evaluate probability calibration via Brier scores and reliability diagrams, quantify clinical utility through decision curve analysis, and operationalise their findings in an eleven-feature clinical decision support system with explicit reference ranges, colour-coded validation logic, and a stateless web-deliverable architecture. The target journal is JMIR Medical Informatics, and the paper follows both the TRIPOD plus AI and PROBAST plus AI reporting frameworks.
articles-methodology-tables: These methodology comparison tables serve a function that goes beyond simple data presentation. They are instruments of scientific transparency, allowing readers, reviewers, and defense committee members to see at a glance how different methodological choices were tested, compared, and ultimately selected across the research articles. What you see here is a structured comparison of approaches across multiple dimensions: the validation strategies employed, the feature sets evaluated, the model configurations tested, and the performance metrics reported. This tabular format transforms what could be a confusing narrative of methodological decisions into a clear, scannable reference that facilitates critical evaluation. Notice how these tables align with TRIPOD reporting standards. The Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis framework specifically calls for clear documentation of study population, predictor definitions, outcome specifications, sample size considerations, missing data handling, model development procedures, and validation methodology. Each row in these comparison tables addresses one or more of those requirements, creating a direct mapping between reporting standards and the research as actually conducted. The tables also facilitate what methodologists call a sensitivity analysis perspective. By presenting results across different configurations side by side, they allow the reader to assess how robust the conclusions are to different analytical choices. If CatBoost performs well only with one specific feature set or validation scheme, that would raise concerns about overfitting to methodological choices. But when strong performance is consistent across multiple reasonable configurations, confidence in the findings increases substantially. For peer reviewers, these tables provide exactly the kind of structured, detailed methodological documentation that enables thorough evaluation. Rather than hunting through narrative text for specific details, reviewers can locate the information they need efficiently, compare it to their own methodological standards, and identify any gaps or concerns. This is scientific communication designed to facilitate, rather than hinder, critical scrutiny.
articles-checklist: This section presents the TRIPOD checklist and PROBAST assessment, and their inclusion here reflects a commitment to research transparency that distinguishes rigorous clinical prediction modeling from ad hoc analysis. Let us unpack what each framework contributes and why they matter for this specific study. TRIPOD, the Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis, is a twenty-two item checklist developed by an international consortium of methodologists, clinicians, and statisticians specifically to improve the reporting quality of prediction model studies.
