A medical informaticist reading an NLP journal needs to know about the pathology parser's architecture and extraction performance. The submission status column tracks each paper through the publication pipeline: manuscript preparation, internal review, submission, peer review, revision, and acceptance. This timeline information helps PhD committees understand the candidate's progress toward peer-reviewed dissemination and provides realistic expectations about publication timelines in medical research, which often extend over many months. The key findings column distills each paper's core contribution into concise statements. For the primary paper, this includes the version two CatBoost area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six and the sixteen-model comparison results. For methodology papers, it highlights specific technical innovations like the NLP parser or the nested cross-validation approach. These concise statements demonstrate that each publication carries its own distinct contribution rather than simply repackaging the same content for different venues. The author contributions column documents each team member's role using standardized contribution taxonomies. This transparency matters both for academic credit assignment and for demonstrating the collaborative nature of interdisciplinary research. Clinical collaborators contributed domain expertise and data access. The candidate contributed the machine learning pipeline, analysis, and writing. Supervisors contributed methodological guidance and manuscript review. The tabular format makes it easy for committee members to assess productivity and impact at a glance. How many papers are in progress? What is the quality of the target venues? Is the candidate the primary contributor? Are the findings sufficiently distinct to justify separate publications? These are exactly the questions that publication summary tables are designed to answer efficiently.
pub-tripod: The TRIPOD statement, which stands for Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis, provides a framework of twenty-two items that prediction model studies should report, and this research's adherence to TRIPOD represents a commitment to methodological transparency that goes beyond what most micro-TESE prediction studies achieve. Understanding how each TRIPOD item is addressed reveals the depth of reporting rigor in this work. The title itself satisfies the first TRIPOD item by clearly indicating that this is a prediction model development and validation study. This might seem trivial, but many prediction model papers use vague titles that do not distinguish between exploratory association studies and genuine prediction model development. A transparent title helps readers and systematic reviewers identify prediction studies efficiently. The description of the study population satisfies several TRIPOD items simultaneously. Two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients with non-obstructive azoospermia from Royan Institute are characterized with clear inclusion and exclusion criteria. The source of data, the dates of the study period, and the clinical setting are all reported, allowing readers to assess the population's relevance to their own clinical context. The outcome definition, successful sperm retrieval during micro-TESE, is reported with precise clarity. TRIPOD requires that the outcome be defined before the study begins and measured without knowledge of predictor values. In this retrospective study, the outcome was determined at the time of surgery by the embryology team, independently of the predictor variables collected preoperatively. Predictor handling receives detailed attention. All fifty-eight features are described, including how the eighteen NLP-extracted pathology features were derived from free-text reports. The handling of continuous predictors, the coding of categorical variables, and the treatment of missing data are all documented, allowing another researcher to reproduce the feature engineering pipeline. Model performance reporting follows TRIPOD's requirements for multiple metrics beyond just the area under the curve. Sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, calibration assessments, and confidence intervals are all reported. The version two CatBoost's area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six is accompanied by its ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five, satisfying TRIPOD's emphasis on uncertainty quantification. The validation methodology using five-by-five nested cross-validation addresses TRIPOD's items on internal validation. The nested design prevents the optimistic bias that affects simpler validation schemes, producing honest performance estimates. PROBAST, the Prediction model Risk Of Bias Assessment Tool, complements TRIPOD by evaluating risk of bias across four domains: participants, predictors, outcome, and analysis. This study's design choices, including the unbiased validation scheme and the transparent reporting of all modeling decisions, aim to minimize risk of bias across all domains. TRIPOD and PROBAST compliance is increasingly required by medical journals for prediction model publications, and this research's adherence positions it favorably for peer review.
ref-knowledge-base: Behind every visualization, every statistic, and every narrative element in this dashboard lies a structured knowledge base that serves as the single source of truth for the entire research program. Think of it as the project brain, a comprehensive repository that contains every methodological decision, every data transformation rule, every model configuration, and every piece of clinical context that shapes this research. The concept of a research knowledge base may seem like an administrative detail, but it addresses a fundamental challenge in complex machine learning research: consistency. When a study involves sixteen models, fifty-eight features, two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients, multiple validation schemes, and dozens of performance metrics, the opportunities for inconsistency are enormous. A number reported in one section might contradict the same number reported elsewhere. A methodological description might subtly differ between the methods section and the supplementary materials. A feature name might be spelled one way in the preprocessing code and another way in the SHAP analysis output. The knowledge base prevents these inconsistencies by establishing a single authoritative record for every fact and decision. When the dashboard reports that the version two CatBoost model achieved an area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six, that number traces directly to a specific entry in the knowledge base, which in turn traces to a specific computation in the validation pipeline. There is no room for transcription errors or version conflicts because every downstream consumer of that information draws from the same source. The knowledge base also captures the reasoning behind decisions, not just the decisions themselves. Why was five-by-five nested cross-validation chosen over other validation schemes? Why were certain features excluded from the final model? Why was CatBoost selected as the champion over other competitive algorithms? These rationale entries ensure that the research can explain not just what was done but why it was done, which is essential for both dissertation defense and peer review. For reproducibility, the knowledge base functions as a detailed laboratory notebook for computational research. Another researcher with access to the knowledge base and the raw data should be able to reproduce every step of the analysis, from preprocessing through model training to SHAP interpretation, and arrive at the same results. This level of reproducibility is the gold standard in computational research but is rarely achieved in practice because most studies rely on informal documentation scattered across code comments, email threads, and meeting notes. The structured knowledge base consolidates all of this into a single, searchable, version-controlled repository that persists beyond the active research phase and serves as a permanent record of the scientific process.
ref-systematic-review: The systematic review of existing micro-TESE prediction literature serves as both the scholarly foundation and the strategic motivation for this entire research program. Understanding what was known before this study began, and more importantly, understanding the limitations of what was known, directly shaped every methodological decision in the current work. The review was conducted following established systematic review methodology. The search strategy targeted multiple databases using combinations of terms related to micro-TESE, testicular sperm extraction, non-obstructive azoospermia, prediction, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. Inclusion criteria selected studies that developed or validated quantitative prediction models for micro-TESE outcomes. Exclusion criteria removed case reports, opinion pieces, studies focused on obstructive azoospermia, and studies that did not develop a formal prediction model. Quality assessment of included studies revealed several recurring patterns that collectively define the limitations of the existing literature. First, sample sizes were generally small. Many studies included fewer than one hundred patients, and some worked with fewer than fifty. These small cohorts limit the complexity of models that can be reliably trained and severely restrict the ability to validate findings on held-out data. Second, model diversity was extremely limited. The vast majority of studies used logistic regression as their only modeling approach, with occasional studies testing a single alternative like support vector machines or random forests. No prior study systematically compared a large number of algorithms on the same dataset, leaving open the question of whether nonlinear models might outperform the linear approaches that dominated the field. Third, the review revealed a conspicuous absence of pathology text mining. Despite the strong clinical evidence that histopathological findings are among the best predictors of micro-TESE outcomes, no prior study had attempted to extract features from free-text pathology reports using natural language processing. Studies either excluded pathology data entirely or relied on manually coded categorical summaries that lost substantial nuance. Fourth, validation methodologies were generally weak. Simple train-test splits and basic k-fold cross-validation were the norm, with no study employing nested cross-validation to separate hyperparameter tuning from performance evaluation. This means that reported performance metrics in the existing literature may be optimistically biased. Fifth, explainability was essentially absent. Prior studies reported performance metrics without providing any insight into how or why their models made predictions. In a clinical context where physician trust is essential for adoption, this opacity represents a significant barrier to translation. These systematic gaps directly motivated every major design decision in the current study: the sixteen-model comparison, the NLP pathology parser, the nested cross-validation scheme, the SHAP explainability analysis, and the clinical decision support system. The systematic review transformed from a scholarly exercise into a strategic roadmap for addressing the field's most pressing methodological needs.
roadmap-overview: Every research program has a narrative arc, a journey from initial conception through iterative development to completion and beyond, and this roadmap traces that arc for the micro-TESE prediction project. Understanding where the project has been, where it stands now, and where it aims to go provides essential context for evaluating both its accomplishments and its potential. The journey began with institutional review board approval under grant identification number four zero two zero eight zero two, which authorized the collection and analysis of patient data from Royan Institute. This foundational step required navigating the ethical requirements of human subjects research, developing data use agreements, and establishing the de-identification protocols that would protect patient privacy throughout the study. Data collection and curation followed, assembling clinical records for two thousand four hundred and thirteen patients with non-obstructive azoospermia who underwent micro-TESE. This phase involved collaboration between clinical teams who understood the medical records and the data science team who understood the computational requirements. The version one model development phase produced an initial LightGBM-based prediction model. This phase was educational in the truest sense, because its reported area under the curve of zero point seven three two seven was later identified as unreliable due to methodological issues in the validation approach, specifically the use of a non-nested validation scheme that allowed data leakage between hyperparameter tuning and performance estimation. The correction of version one's limitations became the catalyst for the far more rigorous version two pipeline. The version two development phase represents the methodological heart of the dissertation. Sixteen machine learning algorithms were evaluated through five-by-five nested cross-validation with Bayesian optimization, producing the champion CatBoost model with its version two area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six and ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. The custom NLP pathology parser was developed during this phase, extracting eighteen features from free-text reports. Comprehensive SHAP analysis provided explainability, and the clinical decision support system translated predictions into practical clinical tools. The current status includes dashboard deployment and defense preparation. The interactive dashboard consolidates all research outputs into an accessible platform, while the publication pipeline advances manuscripts toward peer-reviewed journals. The dissertation document itself is being finalized, weaving together all components into a coherent scholarly narrative. Looking forward, the research roadmap envisions several important next steps. External validation on multi-center cohorts would test whether the model's predictions generalize beyond Royan Institute to other clinical settings with different surgical teams, patient demographics, and pathology reporting standards. A prospective clinical trial could evaluate whether integrating the CDSS into actual clinical workflows improves patient counseling and decision-making outcomes. Integration into Royan Institute's electronic health records would enable real-time prediction during patient consultations. And expansion to other urological prediction tasks would test whether the methodological framework developed here transfers to related clinical problems. Each future direction builds on the foundation established by the current work, and together they define a research trajectory that extends well beyond the doctoral dissertation.
usability-overview: The usability evaluation of this dashboard and its embedded clinical decision support system addresses a question that is absolutely critical for clinical AI tools but too often overlooked in academic research: does the interface actually work for the people who need to use it? The most accurate prediction model in the world is clinically worthless if the urologist cannot figure out how to enter patient data, or if the results are presented in a way that confuses rather than informs. This is not a hypothetical concern. The history of clinical informatics is littered with technically excellent systems that failed at the point of human interaction. The System Usability Scale, commonly known as SUS, was selected as the evaluation methodology because it has been the industry standard for usability assessment since its development in nineteen eighty-six. Despite its age, SUS remains widely used because it is efficient, reliable, and produces results that can be compared against a large normative database. Its ten standardized questions cover the essential dimensions of usability: ease of use, learnability, confidence, and consistency. The evaluators were deliberately drawn from the stakeholder groups who would actually interact with the system in practice. Clinicians assessed whether the interface integrates smoothly into clinical workflows and whether the information presented is clinically meaningful and actionable. Researchers evaluated whether the dashboard accurately and completely presents the research methodology and findings. Technical experts assessed the implementation quality, responsiveness, and architectural decisions. This multi-perspective evaluation ensures that usability findings reflect the genuine experience of diverse user types rather than the opinions of a single homogeneous group. The evaluation was conducted because demonstrating usability is increasingly recognized as essential for clinical AI translation. Regulatory bodies, journal reviewers, and clinical adoption committees all want evidence that AI tools have been evaluated not just for accuracy but for practical usability. A system that scores well on SUS has empirical evidence supporting its readiness for real-world deployment, while identified weaknesses provide a clear roadmap for improvement. The usability evaluation also serves a scholarly purpose within the dissertation. It demonstrates that the research program considered the full translational pipeline from data to deployment, not just the modeling phase. This holistic perspective distinguishes this work from studies that end with a performance metric and leave the question of clinical utility entirely unaddressed.
usability-agent-scores: In a novel twist that adds a meta-analytical layer to the usability evaluation, AI agents were themselves used to assess the dashboard's usability, creating an intriguing situation where artificial intelligence evaluates a platform designed to present artificial intelligence research. This agent-based evaluation approach complements the human evaluator assessments and offers several unique advantages worth understanding. Each evaluating agent was configured to assess specific usability dimensions, creating a systematic coverage of the evaluation space. Navigation clarity was assessed by examining whether a user could intuitively find any piece of information within the dashboard without getting lost or confused. Information architecture was evaluated by examining the logical organization of content: does the structure reflect how users actually think about the research, or does it impose an arbitrary organizational scheme? Visualization effectiveness was assessed by examining whether charts, tables, and interactive displays communicate their intended message clearly and accurately. Clinical workflow integration was evaluated by examining whether the clinical decision support system's input-output flow mirrors how clinicians actually gather and process patient information during consultations. The scoring methodology aggregated agent assessments across these dimensions into quantitative scores that can be compared against established benchmarks. Agent-based evaluation offers advantages that complement human evaluation. Agents can systematically test every navigation path, every interactive element, and every information display in ways that human evaluators might not have the time or patience to do. They can identify broken links, inconsistent formatting, and accessibility issues with mechanical thoroughness. However, agent-based evaluation also has important limitations. Agents cannot fully replicate the clinical intuition that tells a urologist whether a prediction display is trustworthy, nor can they assess the emotional impact of design choices on anxious patients or stressed clinicians. This is precisely why agent-based scores complement rather than replace human evaluations. The aggregate scores reveal the dashboard's overall readiness for clinical use from a systematic usability perspective. Areas where agent scores are high indicate robust design that consistently meets usability standards. Areas where scores are lower highlight specific improvement opportunities that can guide future development iterations. The combination of agent-based systematic evaluation and human expert judgment provides a more comprehensive usability assessment than either approach alone.
usability-strengths: The usability evaluation identified several clear strengths of the dashboard that deserve acknowledgment not as self-congratulation but as documentation of what works well, which is just as important for future development as identifying what needs improvement. Understanding strengths helps ensure that future iterations preserve what is effective while addressing what is not. The intuitive navigation structure emerged as a consistent strength across evaluator groups. Users reported being able to find specific information within the dashboard with minimal hunting or confusion. The navigation architecture follows established conventions for information-heavy web applications, using a clear hierarchy of sections and subsections that maps logically to the research program's structure. Clinicians could navigate to the clinical decision support system directly, researchers could find methodology details efficiently, and technical evaluators could locate system architecture information without difficulty. The data visualizations received positive evaluations for clarity and informativeness. Charts and graphs present complex information, such as model comparison results, SHAP feature importance rankings, and performance metric distributions, in formats that communicate their message at a glance while supporting deeper exploration for users who want more detail. The use of interactive elements allows users to hover over data points for additional context or filter displays to focus on specific aspects of the results. Progressive disclosure was identified as a particular design strength. The dashboard presents summary information first and allows users to drill into greater detail on demand. This approach prevents information overload for users who need a quick overview while providing comprehensive detail for those who need it. A clinician checking a quick prediction does not need to wade through pages of methodology, but a peer reviewer examining the validation approach can access every detail. The information presentation balances comprehensiveness with clarity. Evaluators noted that the dashboard manages to present a substantial amount of research content, including model results, methodology details, explainability analyses, and clinical tools, without becoming overwhelming. Each section maintains focus on its specific topic while providing cross-references to related sections for users who want to explore connections. The responsive design ensures that the dashboard functions effectively across different device types and screen sizes, which is particularly important for clinical settings where physicians may access the system from desktop workstations, tablets, or other devices depending on their workflow context. These identified strengths provide a foundation of design principles that should be maintained and reinforced in future development iterations.
usability-weaknesses: Honestly discussing the identified areas for improvement is arguably more valuable than cataloging strengths, because weaknesses point directly toward actionable development priorities. The willingness to transparently report usability limitations demonstrates research maturity and provides clear direction for future iterations of the dashboard. Information density was flagged as a potential barrier for first-time users. While the progressive disclosure design mitigates this to some degree, evaluators noted that users encountering the dashboard for the first time may feel initially overwhelmed by the breadth of content available. The dashboard presents model comparisons, methodology details, explainability analyses, clinical tools, publication information, and research context all within a single platform, and the sheer volume of available information can create decision paralysis about where to begin. A more guided onboarding experience, perhaps a brief interactive tour or a role-based landing page that directs clinicians to clinical tools and researchers to methodology sections, could address this concern. Some visualizations were identified as needing additional contextual information to be fully interpretable. A chart showing SHAP feature importance values, for example, is immediately meaningful to a machine learning researcher but may require explanatory text for a clinician unfamiliar with SHAP methodology. While the dashboard does provide explanatory content, evaluators suggested that certain complex visualizations could benefit from inline tooltips or brief explanatory annotations that provide context without requiring users to navigate to separate explanatory sections. The clinical decision support system's input form could potentially benefit from smarter default values and more guided data entry. Currently, users must enter all clinical parameters manually, which works but could be streamlined. Suggestions included pre-populating fields with population-level median values that users then adjust, providing input validation that flags unusual values in real time, and organizing input fields in a sequence that mirrors the typical clinical data collection workflow. These weaknesses are specific, actionable, and addressable in future development iterations. None of them represents a fundamental design flaw; rather, they are refinements that would elevate the user experience from good to excellent. Importantly, acknowledging these limitations in the dissertation itself signals to the evaluation committee that the candidate approaches their own work with the same critical eye they bring to evaluating prior literature. This kind of honest self-assessment is a hallmark of scholarly maturity.
usability-sus-form: The System Usability Scale questionnaire merits detailed explanation because it is both the specific instrument used to evaluate this dashboard and a widely applicable tool that anyone in clinical informatics should understand. Developed by John Brooke in nineteen eighty-six, SUS has endured for four decades because it achieves something remarkably difficult: measuring a complex, multidimensional concept like usability with just ten short questions. The ten SUS questions use a five-point Likert scale ranging from strongly disagree to strongly agree, and they alternate between positively and negatively worded statements. This alternation is deliberate. It prevents response bias where a respondent might simply agree with every statement without careful consideration. The positive statements, such as I think that I would like to use this system frequently or I found the system easy to use, are interspersed with negative statements like I found the system unnecessarily complex or I think I would need technical support to use this system. This alternating pattern forces evaluators to actively consider each question rather than defaulting to a uniform response pattern. The scoring calculation transforms raw responses into a single number between zero and one hundred. For odd-numbered questions, which are positively worded, the score contribution is the scale position minus one. For even-numbered questions, which are negatively worded, the contribution is five minus the scale position. The sum of all contributions is then multiplied by two point five to produce the final SUS score. This scoring method accounts for the alternating question direction and produces an intuitive zero-to-one-hundred scale. Interpreting SUS scores requires understanding the normative benchmarks established through decades of use across thousands of products. A score of sixty-eight represents the exact average based on a meta-analysis of over five hundred studies. Scores above sixty-eight indicate above-average usability, while scores below sixty-eight indicate below-average usability. A score above eighty is generally considered excellent, corresponding to roughly the top ten percent of products evaluated with SUS. Scores above ninety are exceptional and rare. For clinical tools, SUS scores carry particular significance because usability directly impacts adoption and patient safety. A clinical decision support system with poor usability might be abandoned by physicians, wasting the entire development investment. Worse, a confusing interface could lead to data entry errors that produce incorrect predictions, potentially affecting clinical decisions. This is why the usability evaluation of the micro-TESE prediction dashboard and its clinical decision support system is not merely an academic exercise but a practical assessment of the system's readiness for clinical deployment. The SUS scores obtained for this dashboard provide empirical evidence about where the system stands relative to established usability benchmarks and highlight specific dimensions where the user experience excels or needs improvement.
usability-podcast: The podcast discussion of usability findings opens a unique window into the evaluation results, one that captures nuances and qualitative insights that tabular score presentations inevitably flatten. When evaluators discuss their experiences with the dashboard in conversational format, they reveal not just what they scored but why they scored it, and these qualitative insights are often more valuable for guiding future development than the numbers alone. The audio format is particularly well-suited for conveying evaluator feedback because usability is inherently experiential. A SUS score of seventy-five tells you the system is above average, but it does not tell you that the evaluator paused for thirty seconds trying to find the SHAP analysis section, or that they found the clinical decision support system's prediction display immediately intuitive, or that they wished the navigation had a search function. These experiential details emerge naturally in conversational discussion and provide the specific, actionable feedback that designers need to improve the interface. Unexpected findings from the evaluation are given space in the podcast that they might not receive in a formal written report. Perhaps an evaluator discovered a use case that the designers had not anticipated, or perhaps a feature that the designers considered secondary turned out to be the one that evaluators valued most highly. These surprises often reveal gaps between designer assumptions and user reality that are crucial to address. Recommendations for improvement are discussed with context and rationale rather than presented as a bare list. When an evaluator suggests that the clinical decision support system should include reference ranges alongside input fields, the podcast allows them to explain the clinical scenario that motivated the suggestion: a physician entering a testosterone value who needs to quickly confirm whether the value falls within the normal range without consulting a separate reference. This contextual framing transforms abstract recommendations into concrete user stories. The podcast section also connects the usability evaluation back to the larger research goal of clinical translation. A model that achieves an area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six is only clinically valuable if it can be delivered through an interface that clinicians actually use. The usability evaluation, discussed in accessible audio format, demonstrates that this research program takes the delivery mechanism as seriously as the underlying algorithm. This connection between technical performance and human factors is essential for the transition from research prototype to clinical tool.
vdefense-hero: Welcome to the virtual defense component of this dashboard, where something quite remarkable is happening. A doctoral candidate in medical informatics is defending a thesis about medical artificial intelligence through the very artificial intelligence system that the thesis describes. Take a moment to appreciate the elegant recursion of this situation. The research is about building intelligent clinical prediction tools, and the defense medium is itself an intelligent interactive platform. This is not merely a stylistic choice; it is a substantive demonstration of capability. Traditional doctoral defenses rely on static slide presentations projected in a room, a format that has served academia well for decades but that imposes inherent limitations on how complex research can be presented and explored. A slide deck is linear, static, and finite. This dashboard is nonlinear, interactive, and expandable. Committee members can explore model results in whatever order interests them, drill into methodology details on demand, interact with the clinical decision support system using test cases, and examine visualizations at their own pace. The virtual defense format leverages every component of the dashboard as a defense tool. The model comparison visualizations allow committee members to explore performance differences across all sixteen algorithms interactively. The SHAP analysis displays enable real-time exploration of feature importance and interaction effects. The clinical decision support system can be demonstrated live with various patient scenarios, showing the committee not just that the model works but how it works in practice. The research methodology is documented with interactive navigation rather than cramped slide bullet points. This approach demonstrates both the research content and the technical capability simultaneously. A committee evaluating a medical informatics dissertation wants evidence that the candidate can not only conduct rigorous research but also build sophisticated software systems. The dashboard itself is that evidence. Every interactive visualization, every responsive layout, every seamlessly integrated component testifies to the technical skills that complement the scientific methodology. The virtual defense also addresses practical considerations. Committee members in different locations can access the defense materials asynchronously, reviewing content at their own pace before or after the synchronous defense session. The persistent nature of the dashboard means that defense materials remain accessible for reference, unlike slides that are typically only available during the presentation itself. Welcome, then, to a defense that practices what it preaches: intelligent, interactive, and designed for the humans who will evaluate it.
vdefense-panels: The defense panel structure maps the traditional doctoral defense format onto the interactive dashboard medium, preserving the scholarly rigor that committees expect while taking advantage of the unique capabilities that an interactive platform provides. Each panel corresponds to a traditional defense segment but transcends the limitations of static slides. The opening statement panel presents the research question and its clinical significance. Here the committee learns why micro-TESE outcome prediction matters: non-obstructive azoospermia affects the most severe end of the male infertility spectrum, micro-TESE offers these patients their best hope of biological fatherhood, yet the overall success rate of approximately thirty-six point seven percent means that accurate prediction could transform patient counseling and clinical decision-making. The clinical stakes are established before any technical methodology is introduced, grounding the entire defense in real patient impact. The methodology panel covers the machine learning pipeline and validation approach in interactive detail. Rather than presenting a single methods slide with compressed text, this panel allows committee members to explore the preprocessing pipeline, the feature engineering decisions, the NLP pathology parser, the sixteen-algorithm comparison framework, and the five-by-five nested cross-validation scheme at whatever level of detail they desire. Bayesian optimization, hyperparameter search spaces, and cross-validation fold assignments can all be examined interactively. This depth of accessible detail supports rigorous methodological questioning, which is exactly what a doctoral defense should enable. The results panel highlights model performance and explainability findings. The version two CatBoost champion model's area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six with its ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five is presented alongside comprehensive comparison with all fifteen other algorithms. SHAP feature importance rankings, dependence plots, and interaction analyses are available for interactive exploration. Committee members can ask about specific features or specific patient subgroups and see relevant visualizations immediately. The clinical translation panel demonstrates the clinical decision support system in action. Committee members can enter hypothetical patient scenarios and observe how the CDSS generates predictions with explainable feature contributions. This live demonstration transforms the defense from a report on completed work into an experience of working technology. Demonstrating the CDSS with clinical scenarios shows the committee that the research has genuine practical utility beyond academic contribution. The question-and-answer session benefits from the interactive medium in ways that traditional defenses cannot match. When a committee member asks about a specific methodological choice or a particular result, the relevant dashboard section can be navigated to immediately, providing visual context for the discussion. This eliminates the common defense scenario where a question requires information from a slide that was presented twenty minutes earlier and is no longer visible. Each panel respects the traditional expectations of doctoral evaluation while demonstrating that those expectations can be met, and exceeded, through innovative presentation technology.
article-a-figure-1: This figure presents the study flow diagram for the entire micro-TESE cohort at the Royan Institute, spanning two thousand seven through twenty twenty-two. The process began with two thousand four hundred fifty consecutive patients diagnosed with non-obstructive azoospermia. After excluding thirty-seven records that lacked the primary outcome variable, the final analytical sample comprised two thousand four hundred thirteen patients. Of these, eight hundred eighty-six achieved successful sperm retrieval, representing thirty-six point seven percent, while one thousand five hundred twenty-seven, or sixty-three point three percent, did not. This gives a class imbalance ratio of roughly one point seven two to one. The cohort further breaks down by attempt type: two thousand two hundred thirty-four were primary micro-TESE attempts, accounting for ninety-one point two percent, and two hundred sixteen were salvage attempts at eight point eight percent. For modeling, the data was split into an eighty-twenty stratified partition, with one thousand nine hundred thirty patients in training and four hundred eighty-three in the held-out test set. An alternative seventy-thirty split of one thousand six hundred eighty-nine to seven hundred twenty-four was used for calibration analyses. Importantly, nine variables were removed entirely due to one hundred percent missingness, including cancer type, cancer treatment, and several hormonal markers like inhibin B and AMH. Partner age was also explicitly excluded because it lacks a direct biological mechanism for predicting sperm retrieval. This rigorous filtering ensures the downstream models rely only on clinically meaningful and available features.
article-a-figure-2: This figure displays the receiver operating characteristic curves for the top five models evaluated on the held-out test set using the version two pipeline. The champion model, CatBoost, achieved an area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six, with a ninety-five percent confidence interval ranging from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. It is plotted in blue and clearly dominates the upper-left region of the plot, indicating simultaneously high sensitivity and specificity at the optimal operating point. Close behind, XGBoost reached zero point eight two one four, LightGBM scored zero point eight two one three, and Random Forest came in at zero point eight one five seven. Logistic Regression, shown in red, trailed at zero point seven nine two six. Each curve includes shaded confidence interval bands derived from one thousand bootstrap resamples, giving a visual sense of the statistical uncertainty around each estimate. The forty-five degree diagonal represents chance-level discrimination. What matters here is that all gradient-boosted tree models substantially outperformed the linear baseline, and CatBoost consistently separated itself as the top performer. This confirms that the non-linear interactions captured by ensemble tree methods are essential for accurately predicting sperm retrieval outcomes in this complex clinical setting.
article-a-figure-3: This figure shows calibration plots, also called reliability diagrams, for the top-performing models. Calibration tells us whether a model's predicted probabilities actually match real-world outcomes. If a model says there is a sixty percent chance of success, we want to see roughly sixty percent of those patients actually succeeding. The x-axis shows the mean predicted probability and the y-axis shows the observed fraction of positive outcomes, binned into deciles. The ideal scenario is a curve that follows the diagonal line from zero to one. LightGBM achieved the best Brier score at zero point two zero one, followed closely by Gradient Boosting at zero point two zero two, XGBoost at zero point two zero six, and CatBoost at zero point two one three. The Stacking Ensemble lagged behind at zero point two two six. LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, and CatBoost all tracked the diagonal closely from the second through the ninth decile, with only mild miscalibration at the extreme high-probability bins. For example, LightGBM showed an observed fraction of zero point seven six six when predicting zero point eight two five, and CatBoost showed zero point seven five four at a predicted zero point nine four one. This means the models are slightly overconfident at the highest probability ranges but are otherwise well-calibrated, which is critical for clinical decision-making where patients and clinicians need to trust the predicted probabilities.
article-a-figure-4: This figure presents the decision curve analysis for the CatBoost version two champion model, comparing it against two default clinical strategies: treat all patients and treat no patients. The x-axis represents the decision threshold, ranging from zero to one, and the y-axis shows net clinical benefit. The key finding is that the CatBoost model's net benefit curve exceeds both reference strategies across the clinically relevant threshold window of approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. This means that using the model to triage candidates for micro-TESE provides genuine added value over simply operating on everyone or no one. A secondary axis shows the net interventions avoided per one hundred patients, quantifying how many unnecessary procedures the model could help prevent. Vertical dashed lines mark the Youden-optimal threshold band between zero point four four four and zero point five zero seven. Why does this matter? In clinical practice, the decision to proceed with micro-TESE involves weighing the probability of finding sperm against the surgical risks and emotional burden. This analysis demonstrates that across a wide range of reasonable probability thresholds, the model consistently outperforms naive strategies, making it a genuinely useful tool for preoperative counseling and shared decision-making between clinicians and patients.
article-a-figure-5: This figure reveals the global feature importance for the CatBoost version two champion model, ranked by mean absolute SHAP values across the entire analytical cohort. The top predictor is luteinizing hormone, or LH, with a mean absolute SHAP value of zero point three one seven. Age follows at zero point two seven zero, then follicle-stimulating hormone at zero point two five zero, and testosterone at zero point two one two. Body weight comes in at zero point one seven four, with Sakamoto left testicular volume at zero point one seven one, BMI at zero point one six nine, and height at zero point one six six. Ultrasound-derived right testicular volume scored zero point one five six, while clinical estimates of left and right testicular volume contributed zero point one two nine and zero point one one one respectively. Seminal plasma pH registered at zero point one two eight. Further down, estradiol contributed zero point zero four three, Sakamoto right volume zero point zero three nine, and prolactin just zero point zero two zero. Notably, histopathology aggregate features and lifestyle or comorbidity flags ranked at or near zero in this preoperative framing. This is important because it reflects the corrected version two feature importance distribution after the version one data leakage was remediated. Partner age was explicitly excluded for lacking biological relevance. The dominance of hormonal markers and testicular volume measurements aligns with established reproductive endocrinology.
article-a-figure-6: This figure is a forest plot comparing the cross-validated area under the curve across all sixteen candidate algorithms from the version one nested cross-validation grid, alongside the held-out performance of the version two corrected champion family. Each model appears as a row with a point estimate and whiskers representing the ninety-five percent confidence interval. The tree ensemble methods cluster together at the top, with LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, CatBoost, and Random Forest all falling between zero point seven one and zero point eight three. The version two corrected CatBoost result is highlighted separately with a star marker at zero point eight three zero six, with a confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five. In the middle tier, models like the multilayer perceptron, Ridge Classifier, K-nearest neighbors, Logistic Regression, and Decision Tree fall between zero point six five and zero point six seven. At the bottom, Elastic Net, Lasso, support vector machine with radial basis function kernel, and Naive Bayes all scored below zero point six zero. A vertical reference line at zero point five marks chance-level discrimination. This comprehensive comparison makes clear that gradient-boosted tree ensembles dramatically outperform both linear models and simpler classifiers for this prediction task, and that CatBoost version two stands as the definitive champion.
article-a-table-1: This table summarizes the cohort characteristics and clinical features for the two thousand four hundred thirteen patients in the analytical sample. The data comes from the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran, and represents a retrospective single-center cohort study that received institutional review board approval with fully de-identified records. The treatment period spans two thousand seven through twenty twenty-two, with the dominant period corresponding to Persian calendar years thirteen ninety-eight through fourteen zero three. This is one of the largest micro-TESE cohorts reported in the literature, which gives the study substantial statistical power. The table documents key demographic and clinical variables collected for each patient, establishing the foundation for all downstream modeling. The single-center design ensures consistency in surgical technique and pathological assessment, though it also means external validation will be needed to confirm generalizability. The long time span of fifteen years captures evolving clinical practices and patient populations, adding both richness and complexity to the dataset. Understanding these baseline characteristics is essential for interpreting the model's predictions and assessing whether the findings might apply to other fertility centers worldwide.
article-a-table-2: This table presents the sixteen-model comparison from the version two corrected pipeline using nested cross-validation. CatBoost earned the top rank with an area under the curve of zero point eight three zero six, a ninety-five percent confidence interval of zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five, accuracy of zero point seven six nine three, sensitivity of zero point six seven eight four, specificity of zero point eight two two zero, F-one score of zero point six eight three two, and a Brier score of zero point two one three. XGBoost placed second with an AUC of zero point eight two one four, followed closely by LightGBM at zero point eight two one three. The gap between these top three models is remarkably small, suggesting that gradient-boosted tree methods as a class are well-suited to this prediction problem. Further down the rankings, the performance drops more substantially. What makes this comparison rigorous is the use of nested cross-validation, which provides an unbiased estimate of generalization performance by separating hyperparameter tuning from performance evaluation. The Brier scores complement the discrimination metrics by assessing calibration quality. CatBoost's combination of the highest AUC with competitive calibration and balanced sensitivity-specificity trade-offs justified its selection as the champion model for clinical deployment.
article-a-table-3: This table details the operating-point performance of the champion CatBoost version two model at the Youden-optimal threshold. The area under the curve stands at zero point eight three zero six with a ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five, representing strong discrimination on held-out test data. The Youden-optimal decision threshold falls in the range of zero point four four four to zero point five zero seven across bootstrap resamples, with a default operating point of zero point five. Overall accuracy reaches seventy-six point nine three percent, meaning roughly three out of four predictions are correct. These metrics matter because they translate the abstract ROC curve into concrete clinical performance. At this threshold, the model balances the trade-off between correctly identifying patients who will have successful retrieval and correctly identifying those who will not. For a clinician counseling a patient before micro-TESE, these numbers provide a quantitative foundation for the conversation. The confidence intervals, derived from extensive resampling, give assurance that the performance estimates are stable and not artifacts of a particular data split.
article-a-table-4: This table places the current study in context by comparing it against ten prior studies that attempted to predict sperm retrieval outcomes in micro-TESE or conventional TESE settings. The comparison is organized chronologically and highlights cohort size, modeling approach, best reported AUC, validation strategy, and key limitations. For example, Zeadna and colleagues in twenty twenty used gradient-boosted trees on just one hundred nineteen patients and achieved an AUC of zero point eight zero seven with internal cross-validation only. Bachelot and colleagues in twenty twenty-three tested eight machine learning models on two hundred one patients plus a small prospective set of twenty-six, with Random Forest reaching zero point nine zero zero, but the prospective test set was very small. Kobayashi and colleagues, also in twenty twenty-three, used an automated machine learning platform on four hundred thirty patients plus twenty external cases, achieving only zero point seven two five. The present study's cohort of two thousand four hundred thirteen patients is substantially larger than any competitor, and the CatBoost version two champion achieves zero point eight three zero six on a properly held-out test set. This combination of scale, rigorous validation, and competitive discrimination represents a meaningful advance over the existing literature.
article-a-table-5: This table describes the eighteen features derived from free-text bilateral pathology reports using natural language processing techniques, along with cohort metadata features. These NLP-engineered variables represent one of the study's methodological innovations. For each testis, the system extracts patterns like Sertoli-cell-only percentage for both right and left sides, labeled SCO RT and SCO LT respectively. Complete spermatogenesis percentages are captured as CSTH RT and CSTH LT. Each feature is encoded as a percentage from zero to one hundred, parsed via regular expressions from the unstructured pathology text. When a pattern is detected without an explicit percentage, a default value of one hundred is assigned. This approach transforms rich but unstructured clinical narratives into quantitative features that machine learning models can consume. The bilateral encoding, treating left and right testes separately, preserves clinically meaningful laterality information. However, in the version two preoperative framing, these histopathology features ranked near zero in importance because pathology results are typically available only after the procedure, not before. This distinction between preoperative and postoperative feature sets is critical for building a model that can actually be used prospectively in clinical practice.
article-a-table-6: This table examines calibration quality and overfitting risk across the top-performing models by comparing cross-validated AUC, training AUC, the overfitting gap between them, and Brier scores. LightGBM shows a cross-validated AUC of zero point seven three two seven against a training AUC of zero point nine zero two six, yielding an overfitting gap of zero point one seven zero and a Brier score of zero point two zero one. Gradient Boosting has the largest gap at zero point two five two, with training AUC reaching zero point nine eight zero seven but cross-validated AUC at only zero point seven two nine one. XGBoost falls in between with a gap of zero point two four zero. These overfitting gaps are informative because they reveal how much each model memorizes the training data versus learning generalizable patterns. A large gap suggests the model may not perform as well on truly new patients. The Brier scores provide a complementary view of calibration quality, with LightGBM achieving the best score at zero point two zero one. This table is essential for understanding why the final champion selection considered not just raw discrimination but also generalization stability and probability calibration, ensuring the chosen model would perform reliably in real clinical settings.
article-b-figure-1: This figure maps the complete analytical pipeline for the explainable AI and clinical decision support system study. Starting from the Royan Institute cohort of two thousand four hundred fifty records screened between two thousand seven and twenty twenty-two, thirty-seven records were excluded for missing outcomes or non-NOA etiology, leaving two thousand four hundred thirteen patients. Of these, eight hundred eighty-six, or thirty-six point seven percent, had successful retrievals, and one thousand five hundred twenty-seven, or sixty-three point three percent, did not. The diagram then splits into two parallel branches. The left branch shows the primary eighty-twenty split with one thousand nine hundred thirty training and four hundred eighty-three test patients, feeding into a sixteen-model nested cross-validation benchmark. The right branch shows the calibration split of one thousand six hundred eighty-nine to seven hundred twenty-four for reliability diagrams and decision curve analysis. Both branches converge at the CatBoost champion model with an AUC of zero point eight three zero six. From there, three explainability methods fan out: global and local TreeSHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence plots. The final step leads to an eleven-feature clinical decision support system specification. The color coding helps distinguish data preparation steps in pale blue, modeling in green, explainability in orange, and the CDSS in purple. This diagram effectively communicates how the study bridges the gap from raw data to an interpretable, clinically deployable tool.
article-b-figure-2: This figure is the global SHAP summary plot for the CatBoost champion model, displaying the top sixteen predictors as a beeswarm visualization. Each dot represents an individual patient's SHAP value for that feature, colored from blue for low feature values to red for high values. Luteinizing hormone leads with a mean absolute SHAP value of zero point three one seven, followed by age at zero point two seven zero, FSH at zero point two five zero, and testosterone at zero point two one two. The hormonal markers show clear directional patterns. For LH and FSH, high values shown in red cluster to the right of the zero line, meaning elevated gonadotropins push the model toward predicting retrieval failure. Testosterone shows the inverse: high values associate with negative SHAP values, meaning higher testosterone favors successful retrieval. Body weight at zero point one seven four, Sakamoto left testicular volume at zero point one seven one, BMI at zero point one six nine, and height at zero point one six six form a second tier of importance. Ultrasound-derived volumes and clinical estimates contribute moderately. At the bottom, estradiol at zero point zero four three, Sakamoto right volume at zero point zero three nine, and prolactin at zero point zero two zero have minimal influence. This visualization is powerful because it simultaneously shows feature importance rankings and the direction of each feature's effect, giving clinicians an intuitive understanding of what drives the model's predictions.
article-b-figure-3: This figure contains four panels showing partial-dependence and local SHAP dependence plots for the top four predictors. Panel A examines luteinizing hormone from zero to forty milli-international units per milliliter. SHAP values rise monotonically from negative zero point four at low LH to about positive zero point four above roughly twelve milli-international units per milliliter, then plateau beyond twenty. This means once LH is sufficiently elevated, further increases add little additional predictive information. Panel B shows age from eighteen to seventy years with a non-monotonic pattern. The most favorable region for predicted success falls between roughly thirty and forty years, with SHAP values rising at both younger and older extremes. Panel C displays FSH from zero to forty, showing a monotonic increase from negative zero point three below eight to roughly positive zero point four five above twenty-five milli-international units per milliliter. Panel D covers testosterone from zero to ten nanograms per milliliter, where SHAP values decrease as testosterone rises up to about four nanograms per milliliter, then plateau. These plots are clinically invaluable because they reveal the exact shape of each predictor's relationship with the outcome, including non-linearities and saturation points that simple correlation analyses would miss entirely.
article-b-figure-4: This figure provides a side-by-side comparison of SHAP and LIME local explanations for a single representative test-set patient, indexed as patient two thousand one hundred sixty-two. This patient had a predicted probability of successful retrieval of zero point seven eight two two and an actual outcome of success. On the left, the SHAP waterfall plot starts from the base value and builds upward. Age contributes positive zero point five six seven at a value of fifty-six years. FSH adds positive zero point four one one at ten point eight milli-international units per milliliter. LH contributes positive zero point one eight one at nine point one seven. Seminal plasma pH adds zero point zero nine nine at seven point eight, and body weight contributes zero point zero eight eight at eighty kilograms. Testosterone partially offsets these at negative zero point zero eight six with a value of two point one nanograms per milliliter. On the right, the LIME explanation shows the same patient as a horizontal bar chart. The top four contributors and their directional signs agree with SHAP, though LIME magnitudes are smaller because it uses a sparse linear surrogate model. This concordance between two fundamentally different explanation methods strengthens confidence that the identified drivers are genuine rather than artifacts of any single interpretability technique.
article-b-figure-5: This figure presents reliability diagrams for the top five models, assessing how well their predicted probabilities match observed outcomes. The diagonal reference line represents perfect calibration. LightGBM, with the best Brier score of zero point two zero one, tracks closest to the diagonal across the full probability range, even reaching an observed fraction of one point zero zero zero in the top decile at a predicted value of zero point nine zero zero. Gradient Boosting at a Brier score of zero point two zero two shows slight under-confidence in the lowest bins but excellent calibration in the middle range. XGBoost at zero point two zero five performs similarly but dips slightly at the zero point eight five bin. CatBoost, the champion model with a Brier score of zero point two one three, is well-calibrated across most of the range but shows a mild downward deflection in the top decile, indicating slight over-confidence at the highest predicted probabilities. The Stacking Ensemble at zero point two two six shows the most pronounced over-confidence, with an observed fraction of zero at the highest predicted bin. A footnote indicates that Platt scaling was evaluated and reduced the Stacking Ensemble's Brier score but did not improve the already well-calibrated tree-based models. For clinical use, this means the CatBoost predictions are trustworthy across most probability ranges.
article-b-figure-6: This figure shows the decision curve analysis comparing five models against the treat-all and treat-none reference strategies across threshold probabilities from zero point zero one to zero point nine nine. All five models, CatBoost, LightGBM, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and the Stacking Ensemble, lie above both reference lines for thresholds from approximately zero point zero five to zero point six zero. At a threshold of zero point zero five, net benefit values range from zero point three two six to zero point three three four across models. At zero point two zero, they range from zero point two zero seven to zero point two two six. At zero point three zero, the range is zero point one zero one to zero point one six one, and at zero point five zero, it narrows to zero point zero four two to zero point zero six one. Beyond a threshold of about zero point six five, all model curves converge to zero. A shaded yellow band highlights the clinically relevant threshold range of zero point two zero to zero point four zero, which corresponds to the probability window most relevant for preoperative counseling. Within this band, using any of the top models to guide decisions provides meaningful net benefit over defaulting to either operating on all patients or none. This directly supports the clinical utility of integrating the model into the micro-TESE decision-making workflow.
article-b-figure-7: This figure illustrates the proposed clinical decision support system workflow in three columns. The first column shows the data entry interface, a patient-side form built with Bootstrap five point three point three containing eleven input fields. These are organized into four groups: a hormonal panel covering FSH, LH, testosterone, estradiol, and inhibin B with an auto-computed testosterone-to-estradiol ratio; an anatomy section for left and right testicular volumes; demographics including age and BMI calculated from height and weight; and a genetics field for karyotype status. Each field uses color-coded validation with red for under-range, teal for in-range, and orange for over-range values. The second column represents the inference engine, a stateless wrapper that applies feature scaling and computes per-feature scores using published scoring logic. Lower-is-better scoring applies to FSH and LH, higher-is-better to testosterone and inhibin B, and centered scoring to estradiol. The third column shows the patient-facing output: a gauge displaying the predicted probability with color bands tied to the clinically useful threshold range of zero point two zero to zero point four zero, a bar chart of the top four feature contributions, and two textual recommendations. One advises discussing probability in context of uncertainty, and the other states that predictions below the threshold band should prompt counseling on alternatives including donor gametes and adoption. A feedback loop arrow indicates recalibration with new local data every twelve months.
article-b-table-1: This table describes the cohort characteristics for the two thousand four hundred thirteen patients in the analytical sample used for the explainability and clinical decision support analysis. The patients were drawn from the Royan Institute in Tehran, Iran, all diagnosed with non-obstructive azoospermia and undergoing their first micro-TESE procedure. The study period spans two thousand seven through twenty twenty-two. This is the same cohort described in the companion modeling paper, ensuring consistency across both publications. The table establishes the demographic and clinical baseline that underpins all subsequent explainability analyses. With eight hundred eighty-six successful retrievals at thirty-six point seven percent and one thousand five hundred twenty-seven failures at sixty-three point three percent, the outcome distribution reflects the challenging reality of micro-TESE, where the majority of patients do not achieve sperm retrieval. Understanding these baseline characteristics is essential for interpreting the SHAP values, LIME explanations, and partial-dependence plots presented later in the paper, as the model's behavior is fundamentally shaped by the population it was trained on.
article-b-table-2: This table ranks the global feature importance for the CatBoost champion model using mean absolute SHAP values, accompanied by clinical interpretations. Luteinizing hormone ranks first at zero point three one seven, reflecting how elevated LH signals testicular failure and hypergonadotropism, making it the strongest non-linear predictor. Age ranks second at zero point two seven zero, with a non-monotonic effect where the relationship between age and retrieval probability is not simply linear. FSH ranks third at zero point two five zero, serving as a classical marker of spermatogenic damage where higher values typically lower expected probability. Testosterone comes fourth at zero point two one two, with higher levels generally favoring retrieval. Body weight at zero point one seven four and testicular volume measurements round out the top tier. The table continues through all sixteen ranked features down to prolactin at zero point zero two zero. What makes this table particularly valuable is the clinical interpretation column, which bridges the gap between abstract SHAP values and actionable medical understanding. Each feature's importance is contextualized within reproductive endocrinology, helping clinicians understand not just that a feature matters, but why it matters from a biological perspective.
article-b-table-3: This table presents the cross-method concordance analysis, comparing how three different explainability techniques, SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence plots, rank and characterize the top predictors. LH is ranked first by both SHAP at zero point three one seven and LIME, with a partial-dependence shape showing monotone increasing failure probability above approximately nine milli-international units per milliliter with a plateau. All three methods agree that LH is the dominant hormonal driver. Age ranks second across both SHAP and LIME, with a non-monotonic partial-dependence shape showing a favorable region between roughly thirty and forty years and unfavorable extremes. FSH ranks third consistently, with monotone increasing failure probability above twelve to fifteen milli-international units per milliliter, confirming its role as a classical NOA marker. This concordance across fundamentally different mathematical approaches is critically important. SHAP uses game-theoretic Shapley values, LIME fits local linear surrogates, and partial-dependence plots marginalize over other features. When all three methods agree on rankings, directions, and non-linear shapes, it provides strong evidence that the identified relationships are genuine biological signals rather than modeling artifacts. This multi-method validation substantially strengthens the case for clinical adoption of the model's predictions.
article-b-table-4: This table specifies the eleven features approved for the clinical decision support system along with their reference ranges, validation color logic, and scoring formulas. FSH uses a reference range of one point five to twelve point four milli-international units per milliliter with a lower-is-better scoring function, where values below twelve point nine score favorably and values above twenty-five score poorly. LH follows the same pattern with a reference range of one point seven to eight point six. Testosterone uses a higher-is-better function with a reference range of three hundred to one thousand nanograms per deciliter, requiring unit harmonization across different laboratory standards. Each feature displays color-coded validation: red for under-range, teal for in-range, and orange for over-range values. The scoring logic translates raw clinical measurements into standardized sub-scores that feed into the overall prediction. This table is the operational blueprint for implementing the CDSS in a clinical setting. By specifying exact thresholds, scoring functions, and validation rules, it enables any fertility center to build a compatible decision support tool. The careful attention to unit harmonization and reference ranges reflects the practical challenges of deploying machine learning models across different clinical laboratories.
article-b-table-5: This table summarizes the probability calibration results for the five top-performing models, reporting both AUC values and Brier scores alongside qualitative calibration assessments. CatBoost, the champion model, achieved an AUC of zero point eight three zero six with a ninety-five percent confidence interval from zero point eight two three to zero point eight four five and a Brier score of zero point two one three. Its calibration is described as showing mild over-confidence only in the highest decile, with acceptable performance across mid-range bins. XGBoost scored an AUC of zero point eight two one four with a Brier score of zero point two zero five, tracking closer to the identity line in the mid-range but with a slight overshoot beyond zero point eight five. LightGBM achieved the best raw Brier score at zero point two zero one with an AUC of zero point eight two one three, showing near-perfect reliability even in the top decile. This table highlights an important nuance: the best-discriminating model is not necessarily the best-calibrated one. CatBoost wins on discrimination but LightGBM wins on calibration. For clinical deployment, both properties matter because clinicians need predictions that are both accurate in ranking patients and trustworthy in their absolute probability estimates.
article-b-table-6: This table provides the numerical backbone of the decision curve analysis, listing net benefit values at representative threshold probabilities for all five models and the treat-all reference strategy. At a threshold of zero point zero five, CatBoost shows a net benefit of zero point three two six while LightGBM, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting, and the Stacking Ensemble range from zero point three three three to zero point three three four, all slightly above the treat-all reference of zero point three three zero. At zero point one zero, the models range from zero point two eight five to zero point three zero zero against a treat-all value of zero point two seven zero. At zero point one five, model values span zero point two five zero to zero point two six three versus treat-all at zero point two one five. The separation between models and the treat-all strategy widens as the threshold increases, demonstrating growing clinical utility at higher decision thresholds. This is particularly relevant in the zero point two zero to zero point four zero range highlighted as the clinically meaningful window for preoperative counseling. These precise numbers allow clinicians and health economists to quantify exactly how much benefit the model provides at any given threshold, supporting evidence-based decisions about whether to adopt the model in their specific clinical context.
article-b-table-7: This table documents how the study addresses key items from the TRIPOD plus AI reporting guidelines, which represent the gold standard for transparent reporting of prediction model studies involving artificial intelligence. The table maps eleven specific TRIPOD plus AI items to the corresponding sections of the paper. For example, item one covers the title, confirming that the paper explicitly states it addresses explainable AI for micro-TESE sperm retrieval prediction. Item three addresses background and objectives, noting that the introduction clearly states the interpretability and clinical decision support system translation objective. Item seven covers the source of data, confirming that the Royan Institute retrospective cohort is described with its time period and sampling methodology. This systematic mapping serves two important purposes. First, it demonstrates methodological rigor by showing that the authors followed established reporting standards rather than selectively presenting results. Second, it makes the paper easier to evaluate for systematic reviews and meta-analyses, where adherence to reporting guidelines is a key quality criterion. For readers assessing whether to trust and potentially adopt the model, this transparency checklist provides confidence that critical methodological details have not been omitted.
